Particularly for women. I'll bet they vouch for me. My mother's political accomplishments are also remarkable. Considering she grew up in a dictatorship. Joining the first Pennsylvania women's political caucus in 1972 to more recently fighting for healthcare and prescription drugs for seniors, my mom has set an example that's hard to follow. In total, she's campaigned in seven successful congressional races as a spouse to two wonderful men. Indeed, my mom's political accomplishments and experience are so varied and so recognized that in 1991, after my father's death, prominent members of The Republican Party urged her to run for the Senate. Of course, none of these achievements define her as my mother. Not through the prism of a son's eyes. I'm blessed to see and know so much more about this remarkable woman. My memories run deep, flashes of her packing my lunch, applying a band-aid, sending me off to college, or serving as a very necessary first line of defense against some aggressive older brothers. And while it's impossible for me to share my full sense of her with you all, let me just say this -- my mother and my heart and mind is a force, spiritual, organic, and loving. Smart, funny, and wise. If, as her son, I can be any two of those things in my life, I'll be lucky. Oh, and by the way, if I look that good at 65, I'll be doubly blessed. But in the meantime, I'm blessed to be a part of her life and her American journey. Mom, I love you. And on behalf of my stepfather, my father, and my dear friend Joe, it gives me great pleasure to introduce the nation to someone I hope and believe will be the next first lady of the United States, Teresa Heinz Kerry. Thank you very much.

speaker: Christie Vilsack
text: When I was 3 years old, my parents took me to meet Harry Truman. When the President Asked to shake my hand, I put my hands behind my back and shook my head no. If I give you this pen, little girl, would you shake my hand, he asked? I wanted the pen, so I took a bribe from the President of the United States. The words on the pen say "I swiped this pen from Harry Truman. " my dad swiped it from me and put it in a safe deposit box for 40 years. When my husband was elected, the first Democratic Governor Of Iowa since 1966, I asked my dad if I could have my pen back, and here it is. My father, an Iowa farm boy and small town lawyer, and my mother, a homemaker and teacher from Missouri, taught me the values that their parents taught them. They were children of the depression and survivors of a world war. They championed the underdog. They thought government should give people in need a helping hand. They taught us the value of hard work. They preached the value of staying out of debt and saving for a rainy day. When it came to accumulating money, my dad said "enough and no more. " the trick, he explained, is figuring out how much is enough. They saved so we could go to college. They taught us the value of community by taking us to church, volunteering, and caring for friends and neighbors. We learned stewardship of our land by planting trees as a legacy for the future. These are the values generations of young men and women in the military have fought to preserve in countries around the world. These are the values we look for when we Elect Public servants at the state and local levels, and these values are the reason I stood for John Kerry in the Iowa caucus in January. >> and that's why I support him now for President of the United States. John Kerry has seen the devastation caused in small midwestern towns when their largest employers pack up and send jobs overseas or across the border. With his plan to give companies incentives to create good paying jobs here at home, John Kerry offers hope to working families, because they deserve a President Who will stand up to the enrons and halliburtons and say enough, but no more. With his plan to ease the burden of health care costs for working families, to provide the same coverage to working families as for members of Congress, John Kerry gives us hope that someday all our children will have access to quality health care, that the greatest generation will finally have a President Who will stand up to the pharmaceutical companies who overcharge and say enough and no more. I have been a teacher for 30 years. Teachers at my middle school are angry that this administration promised to leave no child behind, then pulled the funding to make it happen. They deserve a President Who will stand by his promises to America's children. I taught and now serve on the board of a small midwestern college. John Kerry's service for college program gives hope to those seeking a college degree. By serving their communities, they can earn the educations they deserve but can't afford. Yes, John Kerry embodies the values we Iowans treasure. These values didn't just show up overnight in a well-crafted speech. They began on his parents' knees. He was born at a base in Colorado. His dad volunteered in world war II and when John Grew up, even though many young people were finding ways to avoid serving in Vietnam, he followed the example set by his dad and enlisted. In a dangerous and complicated world, America's President Must lead bravely and wisely by word and deed. John Kerry volunteered for two tours of duty in Vietnam. He saved his small band of brothers when they came under enemy fire. And he risked his own life to save the lives of others. Then I came home and helped lead the fight to end that war -- he came home and helped lead the fight to end that war. In communities across my state, we have sent our sons and daughters off to war. In our hometown, a national guards man was a month from completing his service when he was sent to Afghanistan last week. So far, 14 Iowans taught to believe in the value of honesty and service to country, have died. When Iowans hear John Kerry speak about his service in Vietnam, we feel safer knowing that our next President Has made life and death decisions under fire. I support John Kerry because I want to feel safe again. All of us want to feel safe. Safe in our communities, safe in our homes, safe at work, and safe as we travel at home and abroad. Tom and I have kept our home on Main Street in small town Iowa where we raised our sons. Our friends and neighbors come from all walks of life. Some work long hours at local industries, some work part-time with no insurance at scoint stores, and some care for elderly parents and still find time to coach little league. Some are seniors living on fixed incomes, going without to afford their medicines. Yes, our small towns and rural communities have been hit hard these past four years. But we aren't buying the pessimism of those who say this is as good as it gets. We know we can do better. We embrace the hope and optimism of John Kerry and John Edwards, the conviction that we can build a stronger America, and together, with your hope, we will. And remember, we need enough votes and a little more. Thank you very much.

speaker: David Passafaro
text: Good evening. Since the shot heard around the world, proud bostonians have fought for the protection and preservation of the Democratic process. For nearly four centuries, Boston has stood as a beacon of light for all the world to see. We have been a city of political corporate, scientific and educational revolutions unrivaled around the world. And we continue that legacy this week. I stand before you on behalf of Boston 2004, the host committee for this great convention. Our officers, board of directors, executive director, staff and over 14,000 volunteers have contributed to this effort. We extend a warm welcome to all of you, to delegates an guests of The Democratic National Convention. Welcome to the city of Boston, and the 2004 Democratic national convention. As the first national political convention since the tragedies of September 11, this endeavor has been filled with extraordinary challenges. We thank our friends at the United States secret service, to Boston police department, and the entire group of federal, state and local law enforcement and transportation agencies who worked tirelessly to make this week happen. Equally integral to that task has been the extraordinary efforts and support of our great mayor, Thomas M. Menino. It has been his dream to bring The Democratic National Convention to Boston, and it has been an honor to help to make that dream come true. Everyone fully recognizes the legislative greatness of our senior senator, Edward Kennedy. But to those of us who work with him on this effort, Senator Kennedy was the driving force behind bringing this convention to Boston. The Mayor And the Senator Want all of you to share in this great city. Our history, our diversity, our hospitality and our culture. You have come to our home, the birth place of democracy, to nominate one of our own for President of the United States. The people of the party have given you a sacred trust, and we honor that with our efforts to bring Boston the best convention The Democratic Party has ever seen. We welcome you to our city on the hill and thank you for your support and friendship. Thank you very much.

speaker: Deb Callahan
text: I've got a tough challenge. In three minutes, I need to tell you about a man who spent three decades fighting for the environment. So let me get right to the point. John Kerry will be the strongest environmental President In a century. 35 years ago, John Kerry established himself as an environmental leader. Since then, we've made remarkable progress. Raising environmental awareness, protecting lands, cleaning up our air and water and pioneering new energy technologies. Unfortunately, history is filled with moments when great progress is interrupted. Right now in America, one in eight women of child-bearing age has elevated levels of mercury. 46% of our lakes are not safe for swimming or fishing. Our young people are risking their lives to protect our foreign oil pipeline. But right now in America, we have an opportunity to restore environmental progress. We can Elect A leader who has shown a lifetime of values and vision. John Kerry's leadership is unmatched. He was an environmental leader before it became fashionable. Helping to organize the very first earth day. in the Senate he went on to earn a nearly perfect lifetime environmental voting record. John Kerry's values have never waivered. So often he's faced down the destructive stampede of special interests. He's never allowed the corporate environment to count more than our living environment. John Kerry's vision is needed now more than ever. After risking his own life in war, he's deeply aware of the insecurity our nation faces when foreign oil dictates foreign policy. John Kerry will enlist businesses and workers in a fight for energy innovation, not enlist soldiers in a fight for foreign oil. Developing and implementing new energy technologies is an essential part of John Kerry's plan to create ten million new jobs. And while the private sector's already working on energy innovation, we need a President With the vision to make it a reality. Leadership, values and vision, these things matter. That's why the league of conservation voters endorsed John Kerry and worked to help him secure this nomination. That's why we've launched our strongest presidential election effort ever. I urge you to vote for John Kerry and Elect A true environmental leader. Thank you very much.

speaker: Denise Stokes
text: Good afternoon. I'm from the wonderful city of Atlanta. I have been living with HIV for 22 years. I have spent my entire adult life advocating for prevention, awareness and an end to AIDS discrimination. Former President Clinton appointed me to the presidential advisory council on AIDS in 1995. Today, I'm troubled to see that advisory body has all but lost its voice and effectiveness in this fight. We need sound AIDS policy, not simple prejudice. We live in a free country where gay men and women have the right to be. We live this a -- in a democracy where incarcerated deserve good healthcare and it's never easy to do the right thing. But with the right leadership we can give those who have been -- we can give a voice to those who have not been heard. Like the women of this nation who still don't have affordable female control prevention or even appropriate childcare while we hold our families together in the devastating path of AIDS. And like the children who are becoming infected because their teachers and community leaders are forbidden to talk frankly with them about life and death decisions. Today, people with AIDS are being left homeless, without clean, affordable housing, and we are practicing discriminatory immigration policy. We are compromising the very principles this country was founded on. I have lived in shame but not in shame of being HIV positive. I'm ashamed because AIDS remains an escalating health crisis that affects our economy, our national security, and the stability of emerging democracies around the world. We need to ensure that Americans with HIV are cared for and that we lead the fight globally. This dreaded disease has taken so many loved ones and almost extinguished the hope even in the bravest of us. But we still survive. Gay, straight, young, addicted, old, female, homeless, transgendered, we are all needlessly suffering because so many won't do what's right. But we cannot be led by fear and judgment, and we will not die in vain. My life is at stake. I need a President Who will fight for my survivor and protect my rights. We need real help and we need it now. We need John Kerry as the next President of the United States of America. Thank you.

speaker: Diana DeGette
text: Fellow delegates, friends, and Colorado delegates, we stand on the verge of making possible medical miracles long thought impossible. Thanks to advances in biomedicine, especially embryonic STEM-cell research, we have the opportunity to save the lives of millions of Americans. Who suffer from alzheimer's, parkinson's, diabetes, nerve damage, and other illnesses. STEM-cell research has been embraced by liberals and conservatives. By those who are pro-choice and those who are pro life. Yet, despite this support, hope has been denied to millions for the last three years. Some would rather put politics before sound science, science that can and will improve American lives. We must provide the government support necessary for STEM-cell research instead of heeding the call of political extremism over scientific exploration. Our government was created by and for the people. Manipulation of science for political goals fails that goal. That is why more than 60 of our nation's top scientists, including 48 nobel prize winners, are speaking up. They're warning that we cannot politicize science when we plan policy on global warming, on emergency contraception, and on embryonic STEM-cell research. They see in John Kerry and John Edwards the hope that science will once again come first, that policy will come before politics, and that lives will be saved. Limitations on scientific progress must end, and they must end now. It is time for our government to help, and not hinder, lifesaving medical research. It is time for America to have a President Who puts scientific discovery ahead of political calculation. That President Is John Kerry. As a veteran, a prosecutor, and a senator, John Kerry has shown by example how government service can better the lives of all of our citizens. John Kerry understands that for America to regain its moral leadership in research, we must put the full weight of the government behind scientific discovery. We do not have four more years to wait. America demands leaders who put our nation's best interests ahead of their political self-interest. Only one man in this race has spent his entire career working to make our nation stronger and safer for every American. And that is why, my friends, John Kerry will be the next President of the United States. Thank you.

speaker: Diane Denish
text: Thank you. I am so thrilled to be here today, to be part of this historic convention. The 44th that will nominate the 44th President of the United States, John Kerry. I'm very proud to be one of the first statewide elected officials to elect -- to endorse John Kerry, and I have been so proud to be part and a Chairman Of his campaign in the state of New Mexico. The state of New Mexico that will be delivering all of our crucial Elect Toirl votes to John Kerry and John Edwards in November. Now, we all know John Kerry's resume, and we all know there are so many good reasons to vote for him. Whether it's his courage in Vietnam, or his 20 years of leadership in the Senate, fighting for the interests of veterans and working families. But as a small business owner, as a mother, and now a grandmother, I was inspired by John Kerry's values and his commitment to opportunity. Opportunities for more people to turn their good ideas into thriving small businesses and opportunities for more children to make the most of their talents. We can trust John Kerry with our children's future. In New Mexico, I chair our children's cabinet, which brings together the government officials and our private partners in the hopes of doing one thing -- improving the lives of every single child in our state. We're committed to cutting the red tape that allows too many of our children to fall through the cracks. And John Kerry shares our goals of investing early in our children's lives by providing access to healthcare, prekindergarten education and quality after-school programs. Governor Bill Richardson and I are working hard to do our part in New Mexico. But we need a strong partner in the White House to do right by our children. And that's why we need John Kerry and John Edwards in the White House working for America's children and their families. in the Kerry-Edwards administration, every child will get a chance to have a healthy start. in the Kerry-Edwards administration, every will get a chance to attend a quality public school. And in a Kerry-Edwards administration, all children will have the chance to live their dream. So for our children, let's all work together, day and night, to make sure that on January 20, we are inaugurating John Kerry and John Edwards as President And Vice President of the United States. God bless our children and God bless you.

speaker: Dick Durbin
text: Thank you very much. for the few, the proud, the Democrats serving in the United States Senate, we come to Boston to celebrate the promise of a new strength and a new leadership all across America. But, friends, after the celebration, we must leave with the dedication to Elect John Kerry the President of the United States of America. And we must Elect The Democratic Congress. And we do need a Democratic Senate. Not a Senate with all special interests but a Senate working for the interests of families, businesses, and workers desperate for affordable quality health care. Not a Senate... Federal judges with limited talent but a Senate that helps America develop the talents of a new generation. Not a Senate interested in more tax cut for the wealthy that mortgage our children's future but a sneate working for schools that give kids a chance to succeed. And too often those who serve in the Senate lose these battles by one or two votes. Let me tell you tonight, the first step to the road to a Democratic Senate begins in the land of long prairies where one can stand and see every America in every direction. The first step to the road to a Democratic Senate begins with teh great state of Illinois. Since our last convention, America in my state of Illinois have lost oen of its greatest leaders, Senator Paul Simon. My friend, a great Democrat, a man who brought so many of us into public service. But in Illinois we have a Senate candidate who continues in the great tradition of Paul Simon, a tradition of honesty, a tradition of fiarness. Who is that candidate? You got it. Let me tell you about brack Obama. He is a candidate fot the Senate whose life celebrates the opportunity of America. A candidate for the Senate whose family reflects the hope of an embracing nation. A candidate for the Senate whose values rekindle our faith in a new generation. And a candidate who has extraordinary gift to bring people together of all different backgrounds.I have seen it. I've witnessed. in the 1970's I served a staffer for Paul Simon. I was sent to Cairo, Illinois, a tiny town in the southernmost part of our state, a town taht was in the wake of terrible racial strife. The gulf between blacks and whites gaped wide and almost seemed hopeless. This year I returned cario, Illinois. I found what int the 1970's would have been unthinkable, a gathering of hundreds people, black and white, all wearing campaign buttons for brack Obama. With this election, Illinois, United States Senate, and America will have a man who can help heal the divisions of our nation. The next Senator From the state of Illinois, brack Obama.

speaker: Dick Gephardt
text: My friends, fellow Democrats , fellow Americans, I've known John Kerry for most of my congressional career I've worked had whim. I know his values, I know his heart, and I say to you with complete conviction, there is no one in These United States with more courage, more judgment, more resolve, more of the knowledge and experience to hold the job of President of these United States of America. at the dawn of the global economy, an information age, we need a President Like John Kerry who will turn around the worst job creation record since herbert hoover, at a time when world markets are getting more competitive, John Kerry will put an end to shipping jobs overseas. To special interest bailouts and instead will invest in the skills and education of our people again. At a time of global terror, John Kerry will take on the terrorists where they live before they can threaten us where we live. And he will never pursue a go it alone policy in the foreign policy area that leaves America isolated from its friends and hinders the hunt for our enemies. There's another reason we must win this election and put John Kerry in the White House. Because he will bring with him a great fighter for hard working men and women, Senator John Edwards of North Carolina. In an election year, we hear a lot about the word character. In this election there's a candidate whose character was forged throughout the genuine battlefield heroism, through three purple hearts in the vietnam war. John Kerry defended our freedom at the barrel of a gun. He put himself in personal peril to save the lives of his crew mates. We know he won't take matters of war and peace lightly. John Kerry has the strength to keep America strong, this year, in this election there's a candidate whose character has shown through in the many battles he's taken on for our families. He's one of the first Democrats to take on the deficit. He's a champion of public education and our children's future, John Kerry has been a leader on these issues for decades. And this year, in this election there is a candidate who understands middle class squeeze, who understands what it means for a parent to lose a job, for a family to go without health care or be hit by rising college costs. As some of you know, I've had a single simple purpose for my years in public service, to make it possible for hard working American families to succeed, to help the growing number of families where both parents work, just to pay the bills, where life is a never of ending scramble of credit card debt, where it's a struggle to pass on the right values, to teach simple lessons of discipline and respect, right and wrong to our children. Instead of just helping those at the top, I think we ought to make -- give everyone a chance to reach the top. And if we strengthen all our families, if we make sure our economy enriches everyone, mark my words, the people at the top will do even better. As dr. Martin Luther King taught us well, I can never be what I ought to be until you can be what you ought to be. If a child doesn't have health insurance, we all pay the price when she shows up at the emergency room. If a child drops out of school, we all pay the price of violence, dependence, and I understand jensen. Words can be cheap in a political year. We all know that, but I'm going to borrow some familiar ones, to tell you that we need John Kerry as President Because we need a uniter, not a divider, in the White House. And we need John Kerry as President Because it's time to restore honor and dignity to The White House, to have a President Whose whole life is a testament to his goodness and decency and to his courage under fire. We need John Kerry as President of the United States of America because he will fight for working families and help America to be what it ought to be. Win think back on the months, the miles of my own campaign, my heart is full. You see, I didn't come from much. My dad was a milk truck driver, a proud member of the teamsters. My mom was a secretary. Neither finished high school. I never dreamed I would have the chance to serve so many people in so many ways. I found a true partner in life, my wife jane who I adore, and children, Matt, chrissy, and kate, and their families that I couldn't be prouder of. I had the honor to serve my constituents in Missouri, and a gifted and loyal staff who helped me throughout my career. I'm a lucky man. Together they taught me that win or lose the dream is what matters, the fight is what it's all about, for an America that works for the many, not just for the few, for an America that lifts all those who have been locked out and left out of our bounty and promise. I ran for President Because I wanted to give those people a voice, and I stand proudly at John Kerry's convention because he speaks for them. He strives to lift them up. Their cause is his unyielding concern. To all those who supported me and all those who share my dream for a better America, thank you. May God bless you and may God shine on America.

speaker: Donald Plusquellic
text: Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. I'm so pleased to speak to you on behalf of the mayors of America's cities. As this nation has grown, Americans move to cities in great numbers. In fact, more than 80% of Americans live in a city. Cities in metro areas are where we built parks and libraries and schools. It's where we find the jobs that allow us to support our families. Since 1994, metro areas have generated 87% of the economic growth of this country. That represents over 3. 8 trillion dollars. Our cities generate over 85% of the nation's economic output. Jobs and paychecks. It's a fact that America's metro areas, its cities and suburbs are the engines that drive this nation's economy. However, we're still faced with major challenges. Our latest metro economy's report show during the recent recession t economy lost over three million good-paying manufacturing jobs with health benefits. Now, during this slow recovery, we are regaining jobs. But many of them are lower paying jobs with no health benefits. What does this say about the state of our economy? Lower-wage jobs with no health benefits weaken the overall economic health of our nation. I'm from the great state of Ohio. And it's -- it's a battleground state. I will tell you. We have lost good-paying manufacturing jobs at an alarming rate. Our people are suffering and our cities are feeling their loss. That's why we must invest in cities and metro economies. We must create good jobs that stay here in this country. We must also rebuild our nation's crumbling infrastructure. In fact, the American society of civil engineers recently gave our country's infrastructure a dismal grade. Think about it. Our kids are being driven in school buses over bridges that are in danger of collapsing. Think about the congestion on roads and highways. And think about the millions of hours of lost productivity due to the lack of transent investment. The social and economic prosperity of our nation depends on strengthening our metro economies and strengthening our cities. By investing in cities in metro areas we can create jobs, promote small business development, and create hope and opportunity for millions of Americans. I have a suggestion. While we're rebuilding Baghdad, let's help rebuild Boston and Cleveland and Atlanta and Dallas and Los Angeles and every city in between. As the Mayor Of Akron, Ohio, I believe that Senator Kerry's vision will meet the needs of America's cities and I look forward to working with him as the next President of the United States.

speaker: Doug Palmer
text: My name is douglas palmer. And I'm proud to be the President of the national conference of Democratic mayors. I'm even prouder to be the Mayor Of Trenton, New Jersey, where one of America's greatest revolutionary war victories took place, to win American patriots crossed the Delaware river in small boats and defeated the enemy in a bold, swift attack. Just as another patriot, Lieutenant John Kerry did with the boat and crew he commanded in the vietnam war. My great city of Trenton still knows the duty to fight for our country. We had sent more than 100 of our hometown sons and daughters to fight in Iraq. We pray for their speedy and safe return. We believe in an America that is strong at home and respected in the world. We further believe that America's strength begins in our communities. All across hometown America, our mayors are prepared to work with a new President Who will listen, who will help strengthen our communities, who will support our best efforts and that President Is John Kerry. We are mayors like dom pasquale of akron, Ohio, ready to begin exporting American products instead of American jobs. We are mayors like Martin O'Malley of baltimore, ready to work with a President To fight street crime with more police on the beat. We are mayors like Robert White Houser of east orange, New Jersey, and Kwame Kilpatrick of Detroit, ready to give our children a decent start in life and life long opportunities to earn a decent living. And we are mayors like beverly o'neill of Long Beach, California, protecting the nation's busiest port from terrorism. And Mayor Shirley Franklin of Atlanta, working to solve urban sprawl. In our cities, suburbs and small towns, we're doing our part to build a stronger America. But a stronger America needs strong leaders. Starting with the swift boat commander John Kerry and the mill worker's son, John Edwards. John Kerry and John Edwards are leaders ready to work with our communities, large an small. Ready to give us the tools we need to solve problems, and the hope and optimism it takes to overcome our challenges. And together we will build stronger communities and a stronger country, a nation that truly is strong at home and respected abroad. Thank you and God bless America.

speaker: Earl Blumenauer
text: Good evening. I endorsed John Kerry last November because he is the person who best reflects our values and is best qualified to be president. I watched John Kerry at home and abroad. He follows through. He brings people together as he did with the struggle against the budget deficit and in his tireless work with Republican Senator And fellow Vietnam veteran John McCain, to find the truth about the missing and heal the deep scars from that war. In Oregon and around the country, we struggled daily with the critical issues from Iraq to jobs, from healthcare to our crumbling infrastructure, to the ocean of red ink. These are challenges that John Kerry understands and can handle. The big reason for my early support of John Kerry is because of our mutual commitment to the environment. At a time when our environment is under unprecedented attack, John Kerry has the best environmental record of any candidate in history. We know he will protect our land, air, water, and forest. He will enforce our environmental laws. Using science to shape policy, not politics to shape science. Under John Kerry, park rangers in Yellowstone national park will no longer have to wear respirators because of air pollution. He will work tirelessly to protect our national forests and appoint members of his administration with environmental credentials instead of lobbyists from the industries they're supposed to oversee. John Kerry's environmental strength matters in this election. My fellow oregonians, and, indeed, most Americans, know that the environmental strength is critical to the future of this country. Voters know the environment profoundly impacts more than just our quality of life. John Kerry's energy policy goes beyond conservation. It's about not being held hostage to foreign oil. Protecting the environment is not a burden on our economy. It's an essential part of strengthening the economy today and for the future creating livable communities where our families are safe, healthy, and economically secure. Voters know that a clean environment is a fundamental American value. An essential building block for our future. After winning the most important election of our lifetime, America will be stronger because of the environmental leadership of President John Kerry. Together, together, we can make this happen. Thank you very much. speaker: Edward Kennedy
text: Thank you, bob caro. Thank you, bob caro. Thanks a lot. Thank you. Thank you, bob caro, for that generous introduction. Thank you so much. Thank you very much. Thank you, bob caro, for your generous introduction. And with the continuing support of the people of Massachusetts I intend to stay in this job until I get the hang of it. To my fellow delegates and my fellow Democrats, I've waited a very, very long time to say this. Welcome to my hometown. Welcome to my hometown. To Americans everywhere whose aspirations have been kindled anew by this campaign, we who convene here this evening in liberty's cradle say welcome home. Welcome home for the ideals born in Boston and strengthened by centuries of service and sacrifice. Ideals like freedom and equality and opportunity and fairness and common decency for all. Ideals that all Americans yearn to reclaim. And make no mistake, come November reclaim them we shall by making John Kerry President of the United States. These fundamental ideals light the fire in each of us to do all we can and then more, to see that next January John Kerry has a nice new home at 1600 pennsylvania avenue. It fills me with pride to have our Democratic convention in this city, this hallowed ground that gave birth to these enduring American ideals. Like my grandfather and my brother before me, I have been privileged to serve this place where every street is history's home. The old north church where lanterns signaled Paul revere, the old state house where John Adams said independence was born, the golden steps where waves of new immigrants entered this new land of liberty and opportunity including all eight of my own great-grandparents from Ireland. Here in new england we love our history, and like all Americans we learn from it. We breathe it deep because it sustains us, it guides us, it inspires us. It was no accident that Massachusetts was founded as a commonwealth, a place where authority begins not to a single ruler but to the people themselves, join together for the common good. The old system was based on inequality, loyalty was demanded, never earned. Leaders ruled by fear, by force, by special favors for the few. Under the old unequal system equality of your connections mattered more than the content of your character. Your voices were not heard, your concerns did not matter, your votes did not count. The colonnists knew they could do better just as we know we can do better today. But only if we all work together. Only if we all reach out together, only if we all come together for the common good. Now it is for us the patriots of this new century to do that, to shape our own better future and make it worthy of our past, to choose a leader worthy of our country and that leader is John Kerry. Today more than two centuries after the embattled farmers stood and fired the shot heard round the world, the ideals of our founders still resonate across the globe. Young people in other lands inspired by the liberty we cherish linked arms and sang "we shall overcome. " when the berlin wall fell, when apartheid ended in South Africa, and when the courageous protests took place in tiananmen square. The goals of the American people are every bit as high as they were more than 200 years ago. If America is failing to reach them today, it's not because our ideals need replacing. It's because our President Needs replacing. We bear no ill will. We bear no ill will towards our opponents. In fact we'd be happy to have them over for a polite little tea party. I know just the place right down the road in Boston harbor. For today, like the brave and visionary men and women before us, we are determined to change our government. I have served for many years in the Senate and have seen many elections, but there have been none -- none -- more urgent or more important than this one. Never before have I seen a contrast so sharp or consequences so profound as in the choice we will make for President In 2004. So much of the progress we wauns achieved has been turned back. -- once achieved has been turned back. So much of the goodwill America once enjoyed in the world has been lost. But we are a hopeful nation and our values and our optimism are still burning bright. Those same values and optimism are what brought our forbearers across a harsh ocean and sustained them through many brutal winters that inspired patriotism -- patriots from John Adams to John Kennedy to John Kerry. And their strong belief that America's best days are still ahead. There's a reason why this land was called the American experiment. Dedication to the common good were hard wired into human nature, we would never have had a need for a revolution. If each of us care about the public interests, we wouldn't have had the excesses of enron. We wouldn't have had the abuses of Halliburton. And Vice President Cheney would be retired to an undisclosed location. Thanks to John Kerry and John Edwards he'll have ample time to do just that. Our country demands a great deal from us and we rightfully demand a great deal from our leaders. America is a compact, a bargain, a contract. It says that all of us are connected. Our fates are intertwined. 50 states, one nation. Our constitution binds us together. Yet in our own time there are those who seek to divide us. One community against another. Urban against rural. City against surba -- suburb. Whites against blacks, men against women, straights against gays, Americans against Americans. In these challenging times for our country -- in these fateful times in the world, America needs a genuine uniter not a divider who only claims to be a uniter. We have seen how they rule. They divide and try to conquer. They know the power of the people is weakened when our house is divided. They believe they can't win unless the rest of us lose. We reject that shameful view. The Democratic Party has a different idea, we believe that all of us can win. We believe we are one nation under God indivisible with liberty and justice for all. And when we say all, we mean all. Today in this global age our goal of the common good extends far beyond America's borders. As President Kennedy said in 1963, in his best restraint for nuclear arms, we can help make the world safe for diversity. For in the final analysis our most basic common link is that we all inhabit this small planet. We all breathe the same air. We all cherish our children's future. And we are all mortal. Interdependence defines our world for all our might and all our wealth we know we are only as strong as the bonds we share with others. The dangers of terrorism and nuclear proliferation are our greatest challenges and are shared by all nations. And our greatest opportunities from achieving lasting peace and security to build ago more prosperous society to ending the ravages of disease and the despairs of poverty can all be seized but only if the world works together and only if America helps to lead in the right direction. And John Kerry has the skill, the judgment, and the experience to lead us on that great journey. The eyes of the world were on us and the hearts of the world were with us after September 11. Until this administration broke that trust. We should have honored, not ignored the pledges that we made. We should have strengthened not scorned the alliances that won two world wars and the cold war. Most of us, we should have honored the principle so fundamental that our nation's founders placed it in the very first sentence of the declaration of independence that America must give a decent respect to the opinions of mankind. We failed to do that in Iraq. And more than 900 of our service men and women have already paid the ultimate price. Nearly 6,000 have been wounded in this misguided war. The administration has alienated longtime allies. Instead of making America more secure, they have made us less so. They have made it harder to win the real war on terrorism and the war against al qaeda. And none of this had to happen. How could any President Have possibly squandered the enormous goodwill that flowed to America from across the world after September 11? Most of the world still knows what we can be but only we can be and they want us to be that nation again. America must be a light to the world and under John Kerry and John Edwards that's what America will be. We need a president -- we need a President Who will bind up the nation's wounds. We need a President Who will be a symbol of respect in a world yearning to be at peace again. We need John Kerry as our president. Time and again in America's history we as Democrats have offered new hope of a stronger, fairer, more prosperous future for all our people, a society that feeds the hungry, shelters the homeless, cares for the sick so that none must walk alone. And when the elderly face poverty and sickness that threaten their golden years, we created social security and Medicare. And when the voices of many citizens went unheard and their lives were blighted by bigotry, we fought for equality and justice and for civil rights and voting rights and rights for women and for the cause of America with disabilities. We fought for those. And when higher education was beyond the reach of veterans returning home from the war, we created the g.I. Bill of rights and we have continued ever since to make college more affordable for millions more Americans. And when men and women needed protection in the work place, we demanded safe conditions for their jobs. We insisted on the right to higher pay for working overtime and we guaranteed the right to form a union. And we pledged -- and we pledged -- and we pledged a fair minimum waming so that no one in America who works for a living should have to live in poverty. Only leaders who know this history and abide by the ideals that shaped it deserve to be trusted with our nation's future . Sometimes in recent years they have fooled us with their rhetoric but we will not let them fool us twice. in the White House inscribed on a plaque above the fireplace in the state dining room is a prayer, a simple but powerful prayer of John Adams, the first President To live in that great house. It reads "I pray heaven to bestow the best of blessings on this house and all that shall hereafter inhabit it. May none but the honest and wise ever rule under this roof. " in November we will make those words ring true again. All of us who know John Kerry know that he's a fitting heir to these ideals. I've known John Kerry for three decades. I've known him as a soldier, as a peacemaker, as a prosecutor, as a senator, and as a friend. And in every role he has shown his strengths. He was the right man for every tough task and he is the right leader for this time in our history. John is a war hero who understands that America's strength comes from many sources especially the power of our ideas. He knows that a true leader inspires hope and vanquishes fear. This administration does neither . Instead it brings fear. Fear of rising costs for health care and for college. Fear of higher unemployment and lesser pay. Fear for the future of social security and Medicare. Fear of greater bigotry. Fear of pollution staying on our magnificent natural heritage. Fear of four more years of dreams denied and promises unfilled and progress rolled back. in the depths of the depression franklin roosevelt inspired the nation when he said the only thing we have to fear is fear itself. Today we say the only thing we have to fear is four more years of George Bush. John Kerry offers hope. John Kerry offers hope not fear. The hope of real victory against terrorism and true security at home. Of good health care for all Americans, of social security that is always there for the elderly, of schools that open golden doors of opportunity for all our children, of an economy that works for everyone. That's the kind of America we'll have with John Kerry in the White House. And the roots of that America are planted deep in new england soil. Across this region are burial grounds. Many so humbled you find them without intending to. You're in a town like Concord, Massachusetts, or hancock, New Hampshire, you're visiting the old church there and behind the chapel you find a small plot. Simple stones burying simple markers. The markers say war of 1776. They do not ask for attention but they command it all the same. These are the patriots who won our freedom. These are the first Americans who enlisted in a fight for something larger than themselves , for a shared faith in the future, for a nation that was alive in their hearts but not yet part of their world. They and their fellow patriots won their battle. But the larger battle for fre dom and justice and equality and opportunity is our battle, too. And it's never fully won. Each new generation has to take up the cause. Sometimes with weapons in hand, sometimes armed, only with faith and hope like the marches in Birmingham and in selma four decades ago. Sometimes the fight -- sometimes the fight is waged in Congress or the courts. Sometimes on foreign shores like the battle that called one of my brothers to war in the pacific and another to die in europe. Now it is our turn to take up the cause. Our struggle is not with some monarch named George who inherited the crown although it often seems that way. Our struggle is with the politics of fear and favoritism in our own time in our own country. Our struggle like so many others before is with those who put their own narrow interests ahead of the public interest. We hear echos of past battles in the quiet whisper of the sweetheart deal in the hushed promise of a better break with a better connection. We hear them in the cries of the false patriots who bullied dissenters. These are familiar fights. We fought and we won them before and with John Kerry and John Edwards leading us, we will win them again and again and again and make America stronger at home and respected once more in the world. For centuries kings ruled with what they claimed was divine right. They could not be questioned. They could not be challenged. The people's fate was not their own. But today because of the surpassing wisdom of our founders, the constant courage of the patriots of the past and the shared sacrifice of generations of Americans who kept the faith, the power of America still rests securely in citizens' hands. In our hands. Tru to our highest and noblest ideals we intend to use that power. We will use it wisely and well. We will use it in the poets words, my brother's love to strive to see to find and not to yield. We will use it to heal, to build, to hope, and to dream again. And in doing so we will truly make our country once more America the beautiful. Thank you very much.

speaker: Howard Dean
text: Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you, maine and Vermont. Thank you, Texas, and Michigan. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. All right. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you. Like that one a lot. Thank you very much. I was hoping -- thank you very much. Thank you so much. I was hoping for a reception like this. I was just kind of hoping it was going to be on thursday night instead of tuesday night. I may not be the nominee, but I can tell you this. for the next 100 days, I'll be doing everything that I can to make sure that John Kerry and John Edwards take this country back for the people who built it. Because tonight -- tonight we are all here to represent the Democratic wing of The Democratic Party. I am proud of John Kerry's leadership, and I intend to stand shoulder to shoulder with him as we fight for the things that Harry Truman promised us in 1948. Health insurance for every single American. A jobs program that will create jobs instead of destroy them. Standing up for middle class and working Americans who got a tax increase, not a tax cut. And standing up for a foreign policy that relies on the President of the United States telling the truth to the American people before we send our brave soldiers to fight in a foreign war. I would like a commander in chief who supports our soldiers and supports our veterans. Instead of cutting their hardship pay abroad and cutting their benefits when they get back home. I'm Howard Dean, and I'm voting for John Kerry. I'm voting for John Kerry and John Edwards because I'm tired of seeing hard-working Americans struggling with jobs that pay less than they did four years ago. I'm voting for John Kerry and John Edwards because I want a President And Vice President As good and as strong as the American people. I'm voting for John Kerry and John Edwards because I want to see an America that's restored as the moral leader of the world. America's greatness rested on -- rests afar more than the power of our arms. Our greatness is also measured by our goodness. It's in the capacity of our minds, of our hearts, and it's in the strength of our democracy. As I've traveled America, I've seen that strength. I've seen it in the people I've met, I've seen it in their desire to take our country back for the American people. I saw it in a college student in Pennsylvania who sold her bicycle and sent us a check for $100 with a note that said I sold my bicycle for democracy. I saw it in a woman from Iowa who handed me $50, all in quarters, she saved it from her monthly disability check because she wanted to make America well again. I saw it in a 19-year-old from Alabama who had never been involved in politics before he got in his car and drove to Vermont because he didn't feel like anybody was listening to him in Washington. They learned that politics is too important to be left to politicians. They didn't just pack their bags, pack their hopes that we can take our country back. You know what? We will. We are not going to be afraid to stand up for what we believe in ever again. We are not going to let those who disagree with us shout us down under a banner of false patriotism. We are not going to give up a single voter or a single state because we're going to be proud to call ourselves Democrats, not just here in Boston. We're going to be proud to call ourselves Democrats in Mississippi. We're going to be proud to call ourselves Democrats in Utah and idaho. And we are going to be proud to call ourselves Democrats in Texas. Never again will we ever be ashamed to call ourselves Democrats, never, never, never. We're not going to just change presidents in this election. We're going to change this country and we're going to reclaim the American dream. To everyone who supported me, you've given me so much, and I can't thank you enough. This was never about me. This was about us. This was about giving new life to our party. This was about giving new energy to our democracy and providing hope again for the greatest nation on the face of the earth. And so today, even though you've already given so much, I want to ask you to give one more thing. I want you to give America the President John Kerry. Together, together we can take our country back and only you have the power to make it happen. You have the power. You have the power. You have the power to make it happen. Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Thank you very much.

speaker: Ilana Wexler
text: My name is Ilana wexler, I'm 12 years old and the founder of kids for Kerry. Kids for Kerry is a grassroots organization of kids that supports John Kerry, want to help their futures, and get active in politics.when my parents went to see Teresa Heinz Kerry speak they told me that she was amazing and that they thought John Kerry would make a great president. I decided to find out more about John Kerry. I talk about what I learn by using my Abc's. A is America. John Kerry is a hero to America and he will help our futures. He is a great and positive role model. B is better education. John Kerry wants to make class sizes smaller so that children get the best part out of learning. He wants to help teachers because being a teacher is educating children and letting them out into the world to do good things. C is compassion towards children. John Kerry wants to make sure that health care is not a privilege but is something that we can all afford. This summer I was supposed to go to my favorite camp but instead I decided to go to the John Kerry office every day. Kids can really help. On kidsforkerry. Org we have an interactive presidential quiz. There are 270 questions for the electoral votes. John Kerry will win plus 25 questions to make up for Florida from the last election. Our next goal is to have a petition for national no name calling day, a day that the candidates don't say anything negative about one another. When our Vice President Had a disagreement with a Democratic Senator He used a really bad word. If I said that -- if I said that word, I would be put in a time-out. I think he should be put in a time-out. The last time I saw John Kerry, he grabbed my arm and said, see you at the convention. And here I am. But what will be even more amazing will be the inauguration in January when we have a new President Knowing that kids have made a difference in this election. To summarize, Teresa Heinz Kerry, inspirational, amazing. John Kerry, American hero, next president. Kids -- needs need positive -- kids need positive role models in politics. And our Vice President Deserves a long time-out. Kids, this is about our futures. Make sure all adults you know get out and vote. We can have a voice today! Thank you.

speaker: James Forbes
text: Thank you very much. Presiding officer, delegates, fellow citizens, I am grateful to the Kerry-Edwards team for giving me this opportunity to share a message from an emerging movement of progressive and interfaith religious leaders and people of faith who are joining forces to call America back to her true self. Our message draws inspiration from a set of values rooted in sacred scriptures and reflected in the spirit of America's founding documents. Faith and truth can be found on all sides of the political spectrum. We remember that faith has been a profound force for progress and justice in the history of our nation. From the struggle in regards to slavery and then civil rights and peace. And tonight I bring this message to you. Faith can guide us again. To a stronger and more just America. We seek to restore the moral, spiritual, and Democratic values upon which our nation was built. The progressive faith agenda calls for leaders who will be true to these values. in the tradition of isiah, amos, and other prophets who called nations back to their true selves, we propose a set of prophetic justice principles. These principles call upon our leaders to do the following -- seek the common good, be truthful in facts and motives, promote unity and inclusion, demand justice and care for the vulnerable and the poor, appeal to our higher natures not our greed and our fear, disdain the arrogance of power, cherish Democratic freedoms, respect the human family and the community of nations, ensure stewardship of the earth as God's creation, and, by all means, provide moral leadership. In these challenging times we cry out to our nation "wake up, America. " black, white, brown, rich, poor, middle class, gay, and straight, religious and secular, left, right, and center. We need a healing of our divisions. We must unite to save our nation . Our beloved country -- our beloved America has not been herself lately. After September 11 the peoples of our nation and the world were more united than ever before. The dramatic destruction of that unity since then represents a historic failure of moral leadership. It is up to us to restore the moral, spiritual, and Democratic values upon which our country was built. If we revitalize our commitment to these values we will experience new life together. If we, like the prodigal son, wake up and come to ourselves, we will be able to love America back to health and wholeness. John Kerry and John Edwards are leaders who can rise to these moral challenges. Senator Kerry with his lifetime of service to the common good and Senator Edwards with his consistent commitment to protecting the most vulnerable among us can return America to the principles of prophetic justice and her founding ideals. We all know and sing the first stanza of "America the beautiful." "o beautiful for purple mountain majesties above the fruited plain America, America, God shed his grace on thee. And crown thy good with brotherhood from sea to shining sea. " let us all remember the challenge in the second stanza. "America, America, God mend thine every flaw. Confirm thy soul in self-control , thy liberty in law. " we will stand together. We will work together to make our nation the most beautiful America history has ever known. America America, God shed his grace on thee, and crown thy good with brotherhood from sea to shining sea. God bless you all.

speaker: James Hoffa
text: Thank you very much. Thank you. I'm Jim Hoffa and I'm in Boston this week to cast my vote for John Kerry as a Democratic nominee for President of the United States. I'm a teamster and I'm proud of my fellow union members who make up over 25% of the delegates to this great convention. I'm also proud of the countless union members here in Boston without whom we would not be participating in this great convention. The men and women who built this stage and this great hall that televised this event and basically are out there to protect us are all union members. I'm especially proud of the members of teamsters local 25 who worked the loading docks here at the fleet center, loading and unloading every item that comes into this great hall. I'd like to take a moment to talk about someone who became a member of the teamsters union back in the summer of 1962. He was working at the national warehouse in Summerville, Massachusetts. And was a member of teamsters local 25. He would later become a decorated war hero, a fierce prosecutor and a solid public citizen and a servant to the people of Massachusetts that teamster is John Kerry. I'm proud that John Kerry has a union card. I know he's one of us. I know his record on issues that matter to working families. He believes that every American should have access to quality, affordable healthcare. He believes in healthcare -- that healthcare is a right, not a privilege. He opposes the outsourcing of American jobs, and wants to repeal tax laws that encourage U.S.. Companies to relocate overseas. And he stands with teamsters in our effort to provide meaningful relief for multiemployee pension plans that cover more than 10 million Americans. Unfortunately this administration did not keep its word or commitment to working families. I stand before you today because I know that John Kerry will never leave a worker behind. I know we might not always agree on everything, but I know that the joys, struggles and concerns of the American worker will never leave his mind. And come January, I look forward to gathering with all of you again this time in Washington, d.c.. And at that time, we're going to celebrate the inauguration of President John Kerry and Vice President John Edwards. God Bless America. God bless working families. God bless the teamsters. God Bless America. Thank you very much.

speaker: James Langevin
text: Thank you and welcome. I have been so touched by tonight's speakers and the theme of strength and service. President Ronald Reagan said we have every right to dream heroic dreams. Well, across our nation millions of Americans are pursuing their own heroic dreams by overcoming the challenges of disability and disease. Their community involvement, passion for life, and hope for the future are truly inspiring. When I was 16 an accidental shooting left me paralyzed. Having faced death, I am thankful for every new day and firmly committed to protecting life at every stage. Like our next speaker, my personal experiences lead me with a unique perspective on life, research, and public service and a strong faith that we will find a cure. One promising path to that cure is STEM cell research. And it will take not only the talent and drive of scientists but the support of government to realize its full potential. I believe one day a child with diabetes will no longer face a lifetime of painful shots and tests. I believe one day families will no longer watch in agony as a loved one with parkinson's or alzheimer's gradually declines. And I believe one day I will walk again. STEM cell research offers new dreams to so many people. John Kerry and John Edwards know this, and their support is one more way they'll help improve the quality of life for millions of families. Our next speaker is another heroic dreamer in a time of personal challenge, he's using his experience to make a difference for others. He truly understands the promise of STEM cell research and I am thrilled and honored to introduce Ron Reagan.

speaker: Janet Napolitano
text: Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. A leader, a hero is someone who reaches out a hand when neighbors need help. Someone who has a vision of a better world and brings everyone along. Someone who gives of himself for the good of us all. He's someone who answers the call. He's someone who says "send me. " from a navy gunboat in Vietnam to a prosecutor's office in Massachusetts to the floor of the United States Senate, John Kerry has always answered his country's call to serve, and this year America is calling on him one more time to rebuild the strength of our nation and lift up everyone who is willing to work for the American dream. Today, for millions of our people that dream is like a mirage in the Arizona desert. It's alluring but it's not real. John Kerry will bring the American dream back to life for every American who works hard and does what's right, and he'll start by tackling the rising cost of health care for all our people. Hard work used to be the price of admission to the American dream, but hard work alone is no longer enough to keep up with spiraling health costs. Every day across Arizona and throughout America, people like you and me sit down at the dinner table, long after the children have gone to sleep, and struggle to figure out how to pay their health insurance premiums. Every day millions of older Americans are forced to buy prescription drugs at prices that are devouring their life savings or at times choose between paying for prescription drugs, buying groceries, or paying the rent. And every day people go to work with the knowledge that they're just one serious illness or accident away from financial ruin. America's health care crisis isn't about statistics, and it isn't about politics. It's about people, our families and our neighbors and our friends. And it's time the people got the President And the health care they deserve. It's about a mother in tucson who works full-time to support her children but can't afford health insurance for them, even though cancer runs in her family. It's about a high school basketball player whose future was clouded when he suffered a stroke that has robbed him of his athletic scholarship and left his parents fighting to keep him insured as an adult. It's about businesses having a harder time during -- doing the right thing to provide coverage for their workers. You know something has gone very wrong when starbucks pays more for health care than it does for coffee beans and Ford pays more for health care than it does for steel. Health care cost thes have exploded over the past four years and Americans have had enough. You know, as I've traveled across Arizona, one thing I've noticed is that a lot of people seem to have quit talking about their health insurance problems. I think it's because it makes their blood pressure rise and they can't afford the medicine to bring it back under control. Ready to make a change and John Kerry is ready to make a difference. Because John Kerry knows the daily struggles Americans are facing and John Kerry has a plan. Under John Kerry's plan every American will have access to affordable and reliable health care. He'll do it by cutting through government red tape and by cutting taxes for business that is do the right thing and offer health plans for their employees. That's not just good health care policy. When companies with health plans have an easier time keeping workers on the job that's good for business. That's good for our families. That's good for America. Under John Kerry's plan, every American can get the same high-quality health care that members of Congress have. You and I pay for that health plan. I think it's time we got to be a part of it. And John Kerry will help working families with tax credits so they can afford this care. John Kerry knows healthy children grow up stronger and they perform better in school, and that's why his plan will cover all our children. And John Kerry knows that every senior deserves access to affordable prescription drugs. Now right now seniors can find affordable drugs if they're willing to go to Canada or Mexico. But listen to this, the current administration has actually made it illegal to negotiate lower drug prices on behalf of seniors. Not only will John Kerry allow Medicare to negotiate better drug prices, he'll demand it. And John Kerry trusts us to make decisions about our health and the health of our loved ones. Under his plan medical decisions will be made by doctors and patients not h.m.o. 's and insurance companies. Because it's time Americans stopped being pushed around by their h.m.o. 's and started getting affordable, reliable health care that they need. It's time we gave every American child a healthy start in life. It's time we had a better way to get Americans' health care than using emergency rooms for basic medical needs. It's time our seniors can afford to fill their prescriptions at the corner pharmacy and not in another country. My friends -- my friends, it's time to build a stronger America to renew the American dream and make health care affordable and available for all of us. This November it's time to bring a real American hero, a real leader to The White House. It's time. It's high time to Elect John Kerry President of the United States.

speaker: Jeanne Shaheen
text: It is a very special honor for me to introduce our next speaker. For 18 years in the Senate, tom Daschle has represented the people of south dakota. He has fought tirelessly for the things that they believe in. 10 years ago, his colleagues in the Senate had the good sense to Elect Him as their leader. Ladies and gentlemen, the leader of the Democrats in the United States Senate, a man of values and integrity, Senator Tom Daschle!

speaker: Jeff Bingaman
text: Thank you. First, let me say how proud we in New Mexico are to have Bill Richardson as our governor, Diane denish as our Lieutenant Governor And Bill Richardson as Chairman Of this national convention. Building a strong America begins at home. It begins in states like my home state of New Mexico. The challenge and opportunity that we as Americans have in this election is to get back to that important work of building a stronger America. In America, our great strength is our ability to invent, to create, to innovate. And through that invention and innovation we have created good-paying job, we have built strong communities. The foundation of that innovation begins with our investment in the hard sciences and in technological research. Unfortunately, over the last four years, federal support for research and development in the physical sciences has stagnated. Simply put, U.S.. Leadership in science and technology has not been a priority for this president. And for this administration. And our standing in the world and our ability to compete across the world have suffered. John Kerry knows that unless America retains its leadership in science and technology, the jobs of the future will go elsewhere. In a Kerry administration, the U.S.. Will once again lead the world in science and technology. John Kerry supports efforts to help U.S.. Manufacturers keep jobs here in this country. Today those efforts are being undercut by the current administration. For more than a decade, we have funded a major effort to help U.S.. Technology-based companies to compete in the world market. That's called the nation's advanced technology program. It is a program that work, and it's a program that this administration wants to eliminate. John Kerry supports increased funding for that proven effort. Helping U.S.. Companies to succeed results in good jobs for American workers. It's an essential ingredient for a strong America. And we need a skilled work force to fill those jobs that we're going to create. John Kerry knows that preparation of that work force begins in our schools. Our schools have too often been shortchanged in recent years by over $9 billion just this year alone. Key programs like dropout prevention and smaller schools funded and gifted education and career and technical education are all being short changed. But with John Kerry as President America will once again lead the world in science and technological innovation and our workers will have the skills to take on those jobs of the future. To realize that vision for a better America, join me in electing John Kerry and John Edwards as President And Vice President Of this great country. Thank you.

speaker: Jesse Jackson
text: Evening. Boston, Massachusetts, is the place where patriots and pioneers first started our long March toward freedom and justice. Here the nation began its journey down the road to build a more perfect union. America was founded on the single proposition that all men and women are created equal. It's what makes us strong at home and respected in the world. It is this proposition which is America's promise that each generation has sought to fulfill. for the first time in history, the American Democratic experiment added new rights to its constitution. Among them, free speech, the right to protest and freedom of religion. John Kerry and John Edwards value and are committed to our democracy. Our platform recognizes that voting is the foundation of our democracy. Voting rights are protective of all other rights. Our party is fully committed to protecting the fundamental constitutional right to vote for every American and have it fully counted and fairly counted. No more floridas. Never again. John Kerry and John Edwards are candidates who believe that healthcare is a right for all Americans. And Massachusetts, Senator Kerry's home state, has taken the first step by putting the right to healthcare in its constitution. John Kerry and John Edwards' goal is to fulfill a commitment to our nation's single proposition by providing healthcare of equal high quality for all Americans, including the 44 million Americans who have none. Democrats have a great political team in Kerry-Edwards. During the campaign, John Edwards offered a vision to fulfill the nation's single proposition by vowing to turn a separate and unequal America into one America. The Kerry-Edwards team has a vision of a goal of providing every student with a public education of equal high quality. Kerry-Edwards tla the team to defeat the right wing. They are the right team for new rights. So we may fulfill our nation's single proposition. That all men and women are created equal. Go Kerry and go Edwards. Thank you and God bless you all.

speaker: Jim Stork
text: My fellow Democrats, I'm honored to be here and thank you for the opportunity to speak today. Again, I'm Jim Stork. I'm the Democratic candidate for Congress for the 22nd district of Florida which covers Palm Beach and broward counties. I'm running for Congress for the very same reason all of us are here this week in Boston attending this convention -- we want to return America to the strong, vibrant country we once knew. We not only want change. But we want to be the change. I'm often asked, why did you decide to run for Congress? I just finished a term as the Mayor Of wilton manners, Florida, and I guess the people thought I was done with politics because I wanted to focus on my small business. Let me tell you why I decided to seek elective office again, this time in Congress. I spent the past several months traveling the 64 miles of district 22, everywhere I go, people have been making it clear to me that they're not happy. People of all ages, Democrats, independents, Republicans don't like what's going on in America. They're expressing their frustrations and their fears, they're discouraged and they're uncertain about the future. America's come to a dark place, and they want change. They miss how tall our country once stood in the world. They miss the leadership we had when Democrats were in the White House. They miss the hope and optimism that once united uzz as Americans. Ladies and gentlemen, I don't think we can just sit back and squarely place blame on the party in power because things have gone bad. I believe we must hold all our elected representatives accountable by asking the tough questions. I want to serve in Congress because I think this is a very critical time in our country where we need to ask those tough questions, and I want to be the one to ask them. I want to work to bring people together to find solutions to those tough problems. It's what I did as mayor, and it's what I'll do as a member of Congress. I also want to go to the 109th Congress so I can help President John Kerry and Vice President John Edwards get America back on track. The America we used to know. Thank you.

speaker: Joe Manchin
text: Thank you. Senator Kerry and my fellow Democrats, thank you for giving me this chance to speak to you today. I'm Joe Manchin and I'm running for Governor Of the great state of West Virginia. Some 40 years ago when a determined enemy threatened our nation and our way of life, Massachusetts sent us a leader named John F. Kennedy. My uncle Manchin had the honor of introducing Senator Kennedy when he had the honor of visiting West Virginia during his presidential campaign. He told me the story of how Senator Kennedy appearing at the state capital in Charleston during a rainstorm. While the sun may not always shine in West Virginia, he said, its people always do. I want the people to know of America that under the leadership of another son of Massachusetts, West Virginia, and America will shine again. John Kerry and John Edwards have been to West Virginia many times now. They've traveled our beloved hills and taken the time to meet our people and listened to their stories. And they know like anyone that's ever been to West Virginia knows, that the beauty of our state is rivaled only by the beauty of our people. They know that our people mind the coal that made the steel that helped build this country and made it the envy of the world. They know that West Virginia has sent a higher percentage of its sons and daughters to serve in the military than most any other state in the union. And they know that in West Virginia, our people are proud to give a good day's work for a good day's pay. Asking only for the opportunity to build a better life for their children and grandchildren. West Virginia is committed to electing John Kerry and John Edwards. Because we know that under their leadership, America will be sending our products offshore, not our jobs. We know that under their leadership, the promise that was made to our factory workers, our coal miners and our steelworkers will be kept. For workers who have lost their jobs, for seniors without represcription drug benefits and for children who have been left behind, for every American without health insurance, let us build a stronger America by putting John Kerry and John Edwards in the White House. Thank you. Thank you and God bless you and God save the great state of West Virginia and the united states of America. Thank you.

speaker: John Baldacci
text: Great to be here. Great to be here and great to be from the state of Maine and here in Boston. As New England's only Democratic governor, I welcome you to Boston from The Maine State Convention this spring to our wonderful Maine state reception in Boston this week, I've rarely witnessed the level of excitement and commitment of a presidential candidate like John Kerry. And I'm proud to see that this feeling is shared among our fellow delegates around the country. This Democratic convention mirrors the Kerry-Edwards team. One that's instilling and inspiring hope for a stronger nation. With new and better opportunities for all Americans. We're at defining moment in the history of our country. In Maine, we have not wasted a moment in meeting the challenges that we faced. We have worked hard to create good-paying jobs with benefits. Improved the quality of our public education system. Increasing access to affordable, higher education, and protecting our vital energy and environmental resources. With the leadership of the Democratic legislature, and the last 18 months in Maine, we forged ahead. We have created the health plan ensuring that all have access to universal quality health insurance. We have created a community college system that's opened the doors wider to college than ever before. And we have created the pine tree economic opportunity zones to expand working families and job opportunities throughout Maine. But we have largely had to do this without federal assistance or support. Many states like Maine are financially strapped. And it's obvious that we need a new partner and a new administration in Washington that puts people first, small businesses and working families. With John Kerry as our president, states will have a stronger leader. Will have a share of a stronger America. A stronger economy, a stronger healthcare system, a stronger environment and energy independence and a stronger foreign policy. John Kerry and John Edwards have a plan to move this country forward. Let's carry this message in excitement through to November so that all of America is going to be stronger at home and respected in the world. Thank you very much and God bless, Maine, and God bless The United States. Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: John Dingell
