text: Good evening. My fellow Democrats, and my wonderful friends from our Michigan delegation, who I'm so pleased to see here. And my wonderful wife, debra. Let's talk about something important to every one of us -- our health. And when it comes to our healthcare, America must find a better way. We need to fix the problems in our healthcare system. And we can start by making healthcare accessible to all Americans. Today, 44 million Americans don't have health insurance. At some point, over the past two years, 82 million Americans went without health insurance. That's 1-3 Americans under the age of 65. My friends, that is unacceptable. We must find a better way. Making healthcare accessible means making it affordable. Last year, Americans spent 15% of our national income on healthcare. The average family now pays more than $9,000 a year for healthcare. My friends, that is unacceptable. We must find a better way. And rising healthcare costs are not just a huge burden. To be borne by families. They're mighty drag upon the economy of this nation. I'm from Detroit, and we made cars in Detroit for a long time. And we still make the very best. But I'm not sure how long that will last. Because higher costs make businesses in this country less competitive. Last year, our big three automakers spent $9 billion on healthcare. That number will double by 2010. Today, there's more healthcare costs than steel in an American car. That is also unacceptable. We must relieve American business of a burden which it bears under unfair conditions. We must find a better way. We need to build an America where all Americans can get the quality healthcare they need. And children like young Ethan Bedrick can live healthier and happier lives. Let me tell you that story. Ethan was born with cerebral palsy and when he was only 15 months old, his healthcare plan cut off his speech therapy. According to his plan, they said the therapy wasn't medically necessary. Because Ethan would never be able to speak normally. But Ethan had a good lawyer. Who fought for him and who won. That lawyer was John Edwards, the same John Edwards we'll vote for in November. And when John Kerry and John Edwards are in the White House, working with the Democratic Congress, we'll have leaders who stand up for all of us. We're going to make quality healthcare affordable and available to all Americans. We're going to pass a patients' bill of rights. We're going to work together to build a better and more just America for all of us. Thank you. And go blue.

speaker: John Tanner
text: Thank you. Ladies and gentlemen, I'm pleased to be with you here in Boston. The birthplace of democracy an the cradle of liberty. I want to use this opportunity to alert you to a grave and growing danger that is potentially catastrophic to our country. And precious few of us are thinking about it. I'm talking about America's overwhelming federal debt, some $7 trillion and growing. Our fellow citizens may not realize the extent of this debt problem, of the potential consequences of continued one-party control. Let me give you an example. Last year, the federal government borrowed $374 billion to pay for increased spending and several tax cuts. That was a new record high federal deficit. Where are we this year? We are on schedule to break that record yet again and run up a staggering deficit that is in excess of $450 billion. This out-of-control increase in the federal debt has exposed a very troubling aspect of our current budget policies. Foreign countries -- foreign countries are purchasing our debt in record amounts. These developments leave America vulnerable to a potential national security threat. They drive up interest rates. They increase the cost of borrowing for American families on their mortgages, their car loans, and their kids' education. in the last four years, at least 75% of the increases in the federal debt have been owned by foreign interest and foreign countries. What does this mean? It means that foreign investors , often foreign governments, not U.S.. Citizens, are funding our government's run-away deficits. We cannot be the world's leading economic and military power if government financing depends on funds from foreign countries. Many of whom oppose our policies. No nation in recorded history has ever been strong, free, and bankrupt. John Kerry offers a plan to restore fiscal discipline. John Kerry will enforce budget discipline with spending caps. Listen to this -- financial journalist Alan Sloan wrote in "the Washington post," whose bread I eat, his song I sing. Future generations of Americans may never experience the democracy started here in Boston some 200 years ago if we don't stop now this financial madness. On November 2, we will usher in new leadership with John Kerry and John Edwards. They will fight and restore the ship of state and return this country once again to strength and prosperity. Thank you very much.

speaker: Jon Corzine
text: My fellow Americans, Democrats, especially my friends from the garden state, the great state of New Jersey, are you ready? Are you ready for the countdown to richtry? It's as simple as three, two, one. Three electoral votes, two Senate seats, and one Supreme Court justice. Three electoral votes more than we got last time and will bring John Kerry and John Edwards sworn in next January. Two Senate seats that will make tom Daschle the third majority leader, and together with speaker Nancy Pelosi, will work on the right agenda for America, creating jobs, providing healthcare for all, and making America safe and more secure. And with three and two, we'll be in charge of appointing one, one Supreme Court justice who reflects mainstream values, and a Supreme Court that won't roll back a generation of progress. Women's rights and workers' rights and civil rights. Three, two, and one. They aren't huge numbers. But they will make a huge difference. And all of you are the ones who can make that happen. If the election were held today, we'd not only have President John Kerry and Vice President John Edwards making America stronger and safer, they'd be able to look down pennsylvania avenue and see a Congress willing to work with them, not against them. With your help, Republicans will be handing over the gavel in both houses. But you and I know the election isn't held today. Between now and November 2, they're going to have some advantages. But we have more. Yes, they have more money. But we have better candidates. Like tony knowles in Alaska, inez Tannenbaum in South Carolina, erskine bowles in North Carolina. Not only are we going to win every competed state and every region in the country, but when we Elect Barack Obama in Illinois, Ken Sal azar in Colorado, and Brad Carson in Oklahoma, an African-American, a Mexican-American, and a Native American, we'll have a Senate that proves what we all know -- diversity is our party's hallmark and America's greatest strength. Yes, they'll run more ads, but we have better ideas. We have a simple standard -- are we doing right by America? When you put our great candidates in office, we're going to do right by America. They're going to get out there and make sure America's security is strong, Americans' jobs are in place, and America's future is secure. And while Republicans have to explain the failures of the past four years, we're looking forward to the progress of the next four years. They may have the bully pulpit, but we have the congregation, we have all of you. We have all of you and all of you will make a difference this November. Take back America. Thank you all. God bless you. Let's win in November.

speaker: Kathleen Sebelius
text: Thank you. We're gathered here to celebrate our party, its values and traditions and we're here to celebrate the noble and worthy cause of public service, of giving back to our communities, our states, our nation. Of selflessness and self-sacrifice, it's something that was ingrained in me long before I became governor. A lesson I learned from my father's example. My father, Jack Gilligan, was and is a member of America's greatest generation. Young men drawn to the cause of freedom and justice, by the horrors of hitler's March across europe. Like so many of his generation, my father served his country ably and honorably. I'm proud that for his valor he earned a silver star. That was a different time. Young men across the country were enlisted into service in the pursuit of a global menace, which as many of us remember was not the case two decades later when America went to war again in Vietnam. The cause there was far less certain. The call to service not nearly as resounding. But let us remember that back in those uncertain times, the man who will stand before us thursday night stood up for his country. John Kerry too followed the example of his own father. Putting his countries' interests ahead of his own. When America called, John Kerry marched to the front of the line. He earned a silver star, a bronze star, and three purple hearts. And refused to leave a single man behind. My father returned from world war II and devoted his life to public service. The same was true of John Kerry. Who returned from Vietnam and now seeks the presidency. Determined to ensure America's brightest days are ahead of us. Today, America is calling again, and once again, John Kerry marched to the front of the line. Determined to leave no one behind, as we make America strong at home and respected in the world. The spirit of service and sacrifice we see in John Kerry is something I see in the people of Ohio where I was raised, and in the great state of Kansas whom I proudly serve as their governor. In America's heartland, those values are on display every day. Parents raising their children, teachers nurturing their students, national guard members serving their country, and entire communities pulling together in difficult times of drought or natural disaster. Selflessness and self-sacrifice, the values that define our nominee for president, and the unique character of the country he seeks to lead. Thank you, and God bless you. Thanks very much.

speaker: Kwame Kilpatrick
text: All right, Michigan. My fellow Americans, my fellow Democrats, good evening. I stand before you tonight as an African-American man, a husband, and father of three beautiful children. Throughout this campaign, many have asked what exactly do African-Americans want from the oval office? The answer is simple -- we want what all Americans want. But I stand here representing not only African-Americans, as Mayor Of the city of Detroit, America's 10th-largest economy, a city that moves the world and the global economy, I represent all detroiters, black, white, Arab, Hispanic, Asian, all Americans. I was elected Mayor At age 31 years old, because I dared mighty things for the citizens of my city. The time has come for Democrats to dare mighty things for all Americans. Democrats cannot simply stand on the mountaintop of this country and say what we are against. We must give a clear, direct, articulate message to the people of the United States on what we're for. We stand for a nation that protects jobs with fair-trade policies and trains workers to be leaders in technology and emerging industries. We stand for a country that guarantees all children a world-class education. We stand for a nation that provides health services and quality healthcare to all people, regardless of income and where they live. We stand for a country that refuses to turn back the clock on 40 years of civil rights and anti-discrimination policies. That Americans died for. Like the right to vote and affirmative action. We stand for a safe and secure nation that understands that the war on terror must not be a war on civil liberties. We stand for a country that adequately funds police and firefighters, the brave men and women who keep our cities safe. We stand for a country that invests as much in building its own cities as it does in reconstructing a country halfway across the world. And, yes, we do stand for the values of this country, strong families, healthy communities, personal and civic responsibility, and a commitment to volunteerism and service. It's time, America, to stand up for the men who will lead this country and this nation into the future, men who have the courage to dare to be different. We cannot afford to waste four more years. It's time to stand up for these men right here, right now, it's time, America, to stand up for the next President And Vice President of these United States. John Kerry and John Edwards. God bless you. Thank you.

speaker: Linda Sanchez
text: Good evening. I'm Congresswoman Linda Sanchez, and I am honored to represent the people of the 39th district of California. I know first hand what it's like to live paycheck to paycheck, to struggle to buy your own home, and to wonder how you're going to pay off your college loan. I'm the daughter of Mexican immigrants who came to this country without a dime to their name, no jobs waiting for them, and very little knowledge of the english language. But they believed in the American dream, and worked morning and night to give their children an opportunity for a better life. Every single one of their seven children went to college. Even my mother, at the age of 40, got her diploma. And who would have thought that loretta and I would one day make history as the first two sisters ever elected to The U.S.. Congress. Today, Americans deserve a President Who understands the value of a fair day's pay for a fair day's work. We deserve a President Who is committed to affordable healthcare and affordable prescription drugs. That president, my friends, is John Kerry, and we will Elect Him on November -- in November. We Democrats will win because we know the struggle of the American worker. I've personally been to factories and talked to the men and women who are the backbone of our country. From the time I was 13, my father, a machinist, would take me to work where I saw the pride and integrity in the eyes of the workers. This experience molded me into a civil rights and labor attorney, and I will always remember my conversations with workers on the floor of that shop. That's why my goal is to help working men and women who get shortchanged, denied, and mistreated. I'm here to tell you that, as an advocate, as a woman, as a proud dues-paying member of the idew union, that there's only one candidate in this race who believes that everyone should have a fair shot at the American dream. That man is John Kerry. And if we Elect Him president, I know that he's going to stand for working families. John Kerry is the only pro-worker, pro-family, and pro-job candidate in this race. And only he can make sure that America, in word and deed, will be stronger at home and respected in the world. Thank you so much.

speaker: Lois Murphy
text: Hello. I am Lois Murphy and I'm running for Congress in Pennsylvania's sixth crexal district. I share John Kerry's priorities, job creation, a balanced budget, affordable healthcare, and security at home. And like John Kerry, I'm running for office because most Americans, those of us who worry about the costs of tuition, embraces -- and braces and filling the car with gas to get the kids to school or to get to work, are working harder and harder and falling farther and farther behind. I meet families daily in my district who struggle with soaring medical bills, a sputtering economy, and college twuewigses that are rising fast and far out of reach. Jessica and quinnby whitehawk from exeter are still paying bills from their daughter kayla's three years ago when Jessica was unemployed and uninsured. Don sauter was forced to retire last year when his high-tech plant in redding closed and put 3,000 people in my district out of work. To do right by people like these, we need new leaders in Washington who have the right priorities. It's wrong to create a massive deficit that hurts our economy, takes money from our schools and healthcare, and endangers social security. It's wrong to pass on our debts to our children and our grandchildren. And it's wrong to underfund police and firefighters who will be the first to respond to future terrorist attacks. John Kerry and I have the right priorities. I support John Kerry's plan to cut the deficit because it's the right priority for Pennsylvania families. We must strengthen homeland security. It's the right priority for our safety. We must improve education. It's the right priority for our future. We must create jobs with good wages and good benefits. It's the right priority for a strong economy. And we must reduce the cost of healthcare and prescription drugs. It's the right priority for families. America needs new leaders who have the right priorities to build a better, safer future together, an to make America stronger at home and respected in the world. That's what Pennsylvania families deserve. With John Kerry as President of the United States, and with me as the next member of Congress from Pennsylvania's sixth congressional district, we'll work together to make America strong.

speaker: Maya Angelou
text: Thank you. Good evening. Good evening. Americans. Quote, I question America. Is this America, the land of the free and the home of the brave, where we have to sleep with our telephones taufer hooks because our lives be threatened daily, because we want to live as decent human beings in America?" thank you. Unquote. Fannie lou hamer. It is important that we know that those words came from the mouths of an African-American woman. It is imperative that we know that those words came from the heart of an American. Frederick Douglass. Frederick Douglass, Patrick Henry, all the black men and black women and white men and white women through the years have asked questions of ourselves because in the most private part of the heart of every American lives a burning desire to belong to a great country. To represent a noble-minded country where the mighty do not always crush the weak and the dream of democracy is not in the sole possession of the strong. It is fitting tonight that delegates -- you and I -- hear the questions raised by Fannie Lou Hamer 40 years ago. It is fitting that every American, everywhere, asks herself and himself these questions -- what do I think of my country? What is it in my country which elevates my shareholders and stirs my blood when I hear the words "the united states of America"? Do I praise my country enough? Do I laud my fellow citizens enough? Then there's -- what is there in my country which makes me hang my head and avert my eyes when I hear the words "the yet-to-be united states of America"? What am I doing about it? Am I, like one who sits high and looks low and pretends I see nothing? Am I relating my disappointment to my leaders? And my fellow Americans? As Americans, we should not be afraid to own both the questions and the answers. Down pyramids of years, we have asked these questions of ourselves, and we've had some answers. Patrick Henry said, "I know not what course others may take, but as for me, give me liberty, or give me death. " George Moses Horton, poet, black man, born a slave, said, "alas, and was I born to wear this brutish chain? I must slash the handcuffs from my wrists and live a man again. " the human heart is so delicate. We are so powerful that we need to ask ourselves what do we do in a democracy about making a Democrat si stout and strong and robust? Frederick Douglass said, "the thought of being a creature just of the present and the past was troubling. I longed for a future too. " the love of democracy motivated Harriett tubman to seek and find freedom for herself, but not just for herself. She went back innumerable times to the slave south to gain liberty for slaves, and also to instill in the minds of Americans that it is possible and plausible to own the dream which is freedom and liberty and democracy. Fannie lou hamer and the Mississippi freedom Democratic party were standing on -- on the shoalers of history. When they sought to unseat evil. From its presumed safe perch on the back of all American people. It is fitting that we honor those dreams of the people who thought we can get rid of evil, the evil of ignorance. By seating the Mississippi freedom Democratic party. We are honored that they are with us here tonight. We talked of their gifts to us, their gifts to history. We talked of their gifts to our future. May I ask you, please, let us support and agree and salute the surviving members of the Mississippi freedom Democratic party. Thank you. 40 years ago, the human heart is so delicate and sensitive that it always needs some tangible encouragement to prevent it from faltering in its labor. The human heart is so strong that once encouraged, it beats its rhythm with allowed unswerving -- a loud unswerving insis ten si. One of the things which encourages the human heart is music. Throughout the ages and in all places on this planet, we have created songs to grow by and to live by. We Americans have created music to embolden the hearts of people all over the world. It was encouraging to hear "we shall overcome" sung in tugalog, one of the languages in the philippines. And to hear it sung in France and in Hungary. Ms. Fannie lou hamer knew that she was one woman and only one woman. However, she knew she was an American, and as an American, she had a life that she could shine on the darkness of racism, sexism, ageism, and ignorance. It was a little light, but she aimed it directly at the darkness of ignorance. Her favorite song was a simple song, one we all know. We Americans have sung it since childhood. (music) this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine (music) now, here tonight, we have the fabled freedom singers to lead us in this noble song. Shine, America. (music) this little light of mine shine I'm going to let it shine shine, America this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine let it shine let it shine (music) everywhere I go -- (music) I'm going to let it shine (music) shine, America. This is what we're here for. Let us show that we know how to put a light onto the darkness. Bring John Kerry, bring John Edwards into our lives, into our country, into our White House. Bring them into our lives. Yes, shine them in. Shine! Shine! Shine! (music) this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine oh, this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine let it shine let let it shine everywhere I go I'm going to let it shine oh, everywhere I go I'm going to let it shine everywhere I go and everywhere I go I'm going to let it shine I'm going to let it shine let it shine let it shine let it shine I've got the light of freedom you know that I'm going to let it shine oh, I've got the light of freedom I'm going to let it shine I'm going to let it shine I have the light of freedom I'm going to let it shine oh, let it shine let it shine let it shine Jesus gave me the light I'm going to let it shine oh, Jesus gave me the light I'm going to let it shine Jesus gave me the light I'm going to let it shine let it shine let it shine let it shine I'm going to let it shine oh, all over Florida I'm going to let it shine oh, I'm going to let it shine oh, let it shine let it shine let it shine this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine oh, this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine this little light of mine I'm going to let it shine let it shine let it shine let it shine shine, shine, shine

speaker: Mike Honda
text: Hello, California! Hello America! I love you. You know, 62 years ago American soldiers landed on my parents' California doorstep. They rounded up my family, shipped us to an interment camp in Colorado and incarcerated us behind tall, sharp, barbed wire fences. Our government did this to us and to over 120,000 other loyal Japanese-Americans through a time of war hysteria. Because for us there was no political leadership. Our government told that's we could not be trusted solely because of the color of our skin and the shape of our eyes. They took our homes, they took our belongings, they tore apart our families. All because of racial prejudice. And back then no one in position of leadership had the courage or the strength of character to stand up and condemn the bigotry and hatred leveled against my family. No one. My name is Michael Honda. I am a husband, a father, a peace corps volunteer, a teacher , and a survivor of an American interment camp, a U.S.. Congressman, and a proud, proud American. I am here to support a man for President Whose entire life has been defined by courage and leadership. As chair of the Asian pacific American caucus I support John Kerry because he understands the lessons from the dark chapters of our nation's history. John Kerry has listened to our community's priorities and has made them a part of his campaign. John Kerry is serious about battling the health care disparities, reforming immigration, creating jobs and providing education opportunities for Asian-Americans and for all Americans. John Kerry has spent his entire lifetime fighting for the American dream as a soldier halfway across the world, as a prosecutor, and as our advocate in the U.S.. Senate John Kerry has always lived the gospel of American values. John Kerry's life choices can be summed up in one word -- service. And service is the foundation on which true leadership is built. Answering President Kennedy's call, I learned about service as a peacecorps volunteer in El Salvador. And I know that for John Kerry service is not about self-benefit, it's about self-sacrifice. It's about putting yourself on the line. John Kerry's service has given this country the political leadership we need to ensure national security while fostering international standards of liberty and decency. John Kerry understands the value of international cooperation. He knows that the diversity and inclusion we preach here at home must be practiced in policies abroad. John Kerry will chart a new course for our country, one that points us towards the values of the American dream, one that return us to the principles of fairness and strength. When we Elect John Kerry as our next President And John Edwards as our next Vice President, our country will once again enjoy respect and admiration not just from the world's governments but of the world's people. America will again serve as a beacon of democracy and prosperity as a source of hope, as a model for others yearning to be free. May the spirit of justice continue to bless this Democratic party and The United States of America.

speaker: Nancy Farmer
text: It is such an honor to represent the show-me state today. I have to tell you that I never dreamed that I would be on this stage. I never dreamed that one day I'd be campaigning to become the first woman elected from Missouri to serve in the United States Senate. But that's what we Democrats are all about. Opportunity for everyone. And the chance to succeed. That's what it means to be a Democrat. I know something about opportunity, because I grew up in a working, poor family. My father worked in a grain elevator. My mother cleaned people's homes and took in laundry. I know the needs and wants and dreams of working American families, because I come from one. I also know that right now those dreams aren't just being denied. They're being trampled. From access to healthcare, to unfair tax cuts, to cutting back overtime pay, the middle class and working class in this country are being squeezed. As Missouri state treasurer, I'm all about numbers, so let me paint you this picture of my state. 640,000 people don't have health insurance. 38,000 manufacturering jobs are gone in three years. 31,000 low-income children don't have the no child left behind dollars they were promised. Democrats, we need a change in Missouri. We need a change in America. And, more than anything, we need a leader like John Kerry in the White House. It would have been the highest honor to serve with John Kerry in the Senate, but I can't wait to go to work with him in Washington next year. I know his vision for this country, and I share it. I know his administration will fight every day to make sure our families have a chance to succeed. And I know that if we give families that chance, if we give them the overtime pay they deserve, if we give them a living wage instead of a minuscule wage, if we give them healthcare and the hope of making ends meet, they'll have more than the chance of a lifetime. They'll have the chance for a lifetime of opportunity. Thank you so much for this opportunity. And for this honor. Thank you.

speaker: Nita Lowey
text: Friends, Democrats, Americans, new yorkers, I've witnessed tremendous suffering around the world. I've seen children dying of measles, diarrhea, and other preventable diseases. Families ravaged by AIDS, women with no education, struggling to raise their children. Though they may be half a world away, these are not distant problems. We know all too well that despair breeds radicalism and hatred which will cross borders and oceans. My friends, John Kerry and John Edwards realize that Americans' leadership is essential to meeting these global challenges. John Kerry and John Edwards know that America is strongest when we hold fast to our great and enduring ideals. Fairness, compassion, and justice. These are the principles that should guide all that we do at home and abroad. More than a billion people live on less than $1 a day. And 70% of them are women. John Kerry and John Edwards recognize that when women have opportunities to contribute to the economy, their children are healthier, their families and villages thrive. Loans as small as $15 can jump-start a woman's life, helping her to break the cycle of poverty and give her children the opportunity to lead successful, productive lives. 2/3 of the world's one billion ill literal adults are women. And 60 million girls need primary education. John Kerry and John Edwards will make schooling for girls a priority because they know that education leads to improved health for both mother and child. Reduces child labor and increases political participation. This year alone, 600,000 women will die of preventable pregnancy-related causes. And hundreds of thousands will contract h.I.v. John Kerry and John Edwards will protect women's lives by fully funding the united states' commitment to safe motherhood and family planning. Dramatically increasing our contribution to prevention and treatment. And keeping down the cost of lifesaving drugs. John Kerry and John Edwards know we can and must do more. With their leadership, true American values will guide our policies and the united states of America will be stronger, safer, and once again an inspiration to the world. God bless America.

speaker: Ossie Davis & Ruby Dee
text: For African-Americans, and the south, at mid century, the injustices had become routine. A few -- if you were thirsty, you had to go to a water fountain for colored people. And you had to hope that that fountain was working and it wasn't broken. If you needed a ride, you had to move to the back of the bus. And even if you could afford to travel by car, you had to drive miles out of your way to find a hotel that would take you. Long, terrifying miles, down dark country roads patrolled by the local chapter of the ku klux klan. >> and you had to hope that the single bare light bulb in your motel room hadn't burned out. And if you went to school, you had to get used to learning the same thing every year, because your district had only one teacher. These were some of the experiences of millions of black people including one woman named Fannie Lou Hamer. >> yes, yes, yes. >> born to a sharecropping family in rural Mississippi, she worked on a farm for the first 44 years of her life, toiling under the injustices of Jim Crow. >> but when the civil rights movement came to her town, it stirred in her a desire for something better. ... And from that point on, from that point on, something took hold of her. >> her life was anything but ordinary. >> she endured harassments and physical threats, and despite all that, she registered to vote. ... And for that, she lost her job. >> then she helped others register to vote. And for that, she was arrested and nearly beaten to death on the jailhouse cell floor. >> but she pressed on. She became one of the founders, one of the leaders of the Mississippi freedom Democratic party. >> yes. They came to the Democratic convention in 1964 to protest the disenfranchisement of African-Americans. And the absence of any black in the Mississippi delegation. >> and there, before millions watching on television, she spoke of her ordeal. What she did, she gave a voice to all of us who wanted more, who dreamed of the possibility of ... Better days, she reminded us of our basic values and our purpose, and she helped this nation find its way again. >> 40 years later, we stand before the most diverse convention in the history of the Democratic ... Party. More African-Americans, Asian-Americans, Native Americans, and latinos are here than ever before. >> yes. Yes. >> Fannie Lou Hamer's single decision to stand up and be counted brought us here. >> ... Yes. She guided us, she guided us out of the wilderness of death threats and disenfranchisement, of lynching and literacy tests, of segregation and second-class status. ... One woman from Mississippi did this. One voice lifted so many, all of us. So -- >> so, tonight we pay tribute to Fannie Lou Hamer. >> Fannie Lou Hamer. ... Yes.yes. We honor her courage, her strength, and her refusal to settle for anything less than what was right. Thank you, Fannie Lou Hamer. Thank you.

speaker: Patty Wetterling
text: Hello. I'm Patty wetterling from st. Joseph, Minnesota. I want to begin by thanking my delegates and friends from Minnesota. Thank you. This election day, Americans will go to the polls and make a decision on the most important question of our time. in the shadow of 9/11, are we going to continue to live our lives in fear, or hope? To centuries ago, our forefathers taught us that fear is not an American value. I fought fear since my son, Jacob, was abducted at gunpoint nearly 15 years ago. Our ongoing search for Jacob sent me down a path that was not of my choosing. But I found a path through that dark place. For me, this path has a name, and that name is hope. John Kerry and John Edwards are no strangers to this path of hope. John Kerry's passion for making our world safer was forged in the crucible of combat. And John Edwards' dedication to public service rose from the ashes of personal tragedy. My first steps on this journey were focused solely on getting Jacob back. I have never stopped searching for him. And I never will. But I kept talking to people, asking questions. I became an advocate for children and for families working to change state and federal laws. I hope to take that advocacy to Washington next January. You see, I'm a candidate for Congress in the sixth district of Minnesota. Children and families need more voices of hope. And my work is filled with hope. The story of our nation itself is the story of hope's triumph over fear again and again and again. Think about it. Helen keller. Rosa Parks. John F. Kennedy. Martin Luther King. These great Americans all taught us the power of hope and adversity and the power of dreams combined with courage. This fall, we need to roll up our sleeves and work together toward victory. It's time to translate our commitment into policies that banish fear and encourage hope to thrive. November 2, let's put our words into action and vote for hope. Thank you very much. Thank you.

speaker: Reg Weaver
text: I'm Reg Weaver. Tonight, I speak to you not only as a public schoolteacher, but also on behalf of 2. 7 million members of The National Education Association. Ordinary people who do extra ordinary things, in public schools every day. Touching the lives of over 40 million children. Public education isn't just the key to opportunity. Or economic security. It is the key to America's future. And that is why every child in America regardless of race or creed or color deserves the right to attend a great neighborhood public school. A school with qualified teachers, small class size, parental and community involvement and adequate and equitable funding. A school that has high expectations and embraces shared accountability. A school that is safe and inspires excellent teaching. A school that is alive with learning where every child flourishes. This isn't an unattainable dream. It is our dream. Our commitment. A goal and a commitment that John Kerry and John Edwards share and know how to make it a reality. John Kerry is determined to reduce class sizes. To establish universal preschool. To recite a half a million new teachers and to reject voucher, raise teacher salaries an ensure affordable healthcare for all Americans. From America's teachers and education support professionals, John Kerry is an -- gets an a, and today, many of our public schools are straining under the so-called no child left behind law. This law is underfunded and threatens to leave even more children behind. It is a one size fits all federal mandate that sets the federal mandate that sets the wrong priorities. Our schools are becoming testing factories, not centers of learning an progress. John Kerry will lead the fight to fix and fund this law. He knows that teaching children to test well is not the same as teaching them to read, to compute, and to think well. 50 years ago, The Supreme Court proclaimed in brown v. The board of education that every child, every child in America, was entitled to a quality, public education, and that promise has yet to be fulfilled. But we can make that promise a reality, and we can make great public schools for every child in America, and it starts in November, when we Elect John Kerry, President of the United States of America.

speaker: Robert Caro
text: It is truly an honor to introduce to this convention Edward M. Kennedy. He is a member of a family which has written so many shining chapters in American history, and during his own four decades in the United States Senate he has been writing quietly but with great statesmanship and courage a shining chapter of his own. It is especially an honor for me because for many years I have been watching ted Kennedy in the Senate. In my last book I wanted to write about the Senate and its history and its power and in order to get a feeling for the institution itself, its moods and its customs, I would sit week after week in the Senate gallery and its committee rooms trying to absorb how it works. And it was while I was doing that that I came slowly and almost by accident to the realization of how much Edward Kennedy has meant to the Senate and to America. The Senate was, of course, created by the founding fathers to be a mighty institution with great power. During the 19th century, during the past, the Senate exercised this power creating monumental pieces of legislation that shaped the young nation. And during the 19th century, during that past, the Senate produced monumental figures, Harry Clay, John Calhoun, and two great senators from here in Massachusetts, Daniel Webster and Charles Sumner. Trying to understand the Senate -- to get a feeling for it I sat in the gallery. And because I was thinking about its history and how I would write about that history, my mind was focused on the past, on the great senators of the past. But as I sat there week after week I slowly came to realize that I was also watching a great Senator Of the present, Edward M. Kennedy of Massachusetts. In debate after debate I came to see, Edward Kennedy's voice was the voice of principle, the voice that echoed back to what the founding fathers intended the Senate to be. You know, when Lyndon Johnson as leader of the Senate was trying to pass a bill that really mattered, that would change the course of American history, he would try to make a young senator, or for that matter an old senator, understand that on this particular issue he must put aside the interests of his state, the interests of his section, and think instead of the interests of the nation as a whole. And Lyndon Johnson would say to the senator, you've got to be a Senator of the United States, the whole united states. Sitting there in the Senate gallery week after week watching ted Kennedy fight for great causes, the causes that transcended a state or a section that phrase came back to me over and over again because I saw that's what ted Kennedy was, a Senator of the United States. His allegiance was invariably always to principle, to great causes. Think of what ted Kennedy has stood for, of the legion of causes that he has championed during his 42 years in the Senate. Health care, education, the rights of working families, helping to end the war in Vietnam, apartheid in South Africa, and violence in Northern Ireland. Ted Kennedy has spoken of civil rights as America's great unfinished business. Well, no one has done more to try to finish it. When you think back on the struggle for fair housing, for legislation, the struggle to protect not only African-Americans but women, the disabled, the ainled, when you think back on the score of such causes, you realize ted Kennedy has always been the champion they could count on. It's not easy to champion such causes. To champion social justice, decade after decade particularly when the political climate of the time makes those causes controversial, even unpopular. Sometimes takes a great deal of bravery to do that. But Edward Kennedy's championship of these causes has never faultered. His brother, President John F. Kennedy, wrote a famous book, "profiles in courage. " Edward Kennedy's four decades in the United States Senate, his 42 years in the Senate have been a profile in courage. If you are a historian, you realize as you look back over the long sweep of American history a few individuals have left a mark on that history that will truly endure. Because much the brilliance and the forcefulness and the steadfastness in which Edward Kennedy has championed the great causes of our time, because of the way he has brought them into and kept them in the national dialogue, because of the way he has made America care about them, the mark that Edward Kennedy has left on American history will endure as long as that history endures. It's a great honor now to meet a nd welcome him to this convention, Senator Edward M. Kennedy!

speaker: Robert Matsui
text: Thank you. When I was a young man, I wanted to be an architect. And the reason I wanted to be an architect is because I obviously wanted to build things. As you can see, I ended up on a different path. But I'm still working to build something. With your help, I want to build a new Democratic majority. A new majority that brings with it a new commitment to America's security, a new majority that brings passion to the families who are looking for good jobs, with good benefits. A new majority that shares our commitment to providing America's children an opportunity to attend good public schools, and then go on to college. And of course guaranteing our seniors a retirement with dignity after a lifetime of hard work. A new majority that makes the house of representatives, once again, the people's house. I have no doubt that we are going to make John Kerry and John Edwards the next President And Vice President of the United States. And when they report to work at the White House, I want them sending their policies, America's priorities, down pennsylvania avenue, to the first woman speaker of the house. Nancy Pelosi. Just think about this. President Kerry, Vice President Edwards, speaker Pelosi, and majority leader, Daschle. These are the leaders who will speak for all Americans, will work for all Americans and build a better America. What do we need to do to make this new majority? Well, just two seats in the United States Senate, just 11 in the house of representatives, and one new -- one new President of the United States. And already we're seeing positive signs of a majority in the making. Just look at this year's special elections. In South Dakota, a state where President Bush received 60% of the vote, Stephanie, a Democrat, won. In Kentucky, a state where George Bush received 60% of the vote, a Democrat, chand letter, won. And there is a reason why we Democrats are winning. It's because people don't care about red states or blue states. They care about a stronger America. They care about an expanded economy and brighter future. That's what we intend to provide. We've got the best organization, the best candidates, and the best ideas. And together, we will build a new majority. Thank you.

speaker: Ron Reagan
text: Thank you. Thank you very much. That's very kind. Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. A few of you may be surprised to see someone with my last name showing up to speak at a Democratic convention. Apparently some of you are not. Let me assure you I am not here to make a political speech and the topic at hand should not, must not have anything to do with partisanship. I am here tonight to talk about the issue of research into what may be the greatest medical breakthrough in our or any lifetime, the use of embryonic STEM cells, cells created using the material of our own bodies to cure a wide range of fatal and debilitating illnesses, parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, diabetes, Lymphomas, spinal cord injuries and much more. Millions are afflicted and every year, every day, tragedy is visited upon families across the country around the world. Now it may be within our power to put an end to this suffering. We only need to try. Some of you -- some of you already know what I'm talking about when I say embryonic cell research. Others are probably thinking that's quite a mouthful. Maybe this is a good time to go for a tall cold one. Well wait a minute. Let me try to paint as simple a picture as I can while still doing justice to the science, the incredible science involved. Let's say that 10 or so years from now you are diagnosed with parkinson's disease. There is currently no cure and drug therapy with its side effects can only temporarily relieve the symptoms. Now imagine going to a doctor and instead of prescribing drugs takes a few skin cells from your arm. The nucleus of one of your cells is placed into a donor egg whose own nucleus has been removed, a bit of chemical or electrical stimulation will encourage your cell's nucleus to begin dividing creating new cells which will then be placed into a tissue culture. Those cells will generate embryonic STEM cells containing only your d.n.a. Thereby eliminating the risk of tissue rejection. The STEM cells are then driven to become the very neuro cells that are deficient in Park Inson's patients and finally those cells with your d.n.a. Are injected into your brain where they will replace the faulty cells whose failure to produce adequate dopamine led to parkinson's disease in the first place. In other words, you're cured. And another thing -- another thing, these embryonic STEM cells, they could continue to replicate indefinitely and theoretically can be induced to re-create virtually any tissue in your body. How would you like to have your own personal biological repair kit standing by at the hospital? Sounds like magic? Welcome to the future of medicine. By the way, no fetal tissue is involved in this process. No fetuses are created. None destroyed. This all happens in the laboratory at the cellular level. Now there are those who would stand in the way of this remarkable future, who would deny the federal funding so crucial to basic research. They argue that interfering with the development of even the earliest stage embryo, even one that will never be implanted in a womb and will never develop into an actual fetus is tantamount to murder. A few of these folks, needless to say, are just grinding a political ax and they should be ashamed of themselves. Many are well meaning and sin veer. Their belief is just that, an article of faith and they are entitled to it but it does not follow that the theology of a few should be allowed to forestall the health and well-being of the many. And how can we affirm life if we abandon those whose own lives are so desperately at-risk? It is a hallmark of human intelligence that we are able to make distinctions. Yes, these cells could theoretically have the potential under very different circumstances to develop into human beings. That potential is where their magic lies but they are not in and of themselves human beings. They have no fingers and toes, no brain or spinal cord, they have no thoughts, no fears, they feel no pain. Surely we can distinguish between these undifferentiated cells multiplying in a tissue culture and a living, breathing person, a parent, a spouse, a child. I know a child -- well, she must be 13 now. I guess I'd better call her a young woman. She has fingers and toes. She has a mind. She has memories. She has hopes. She has juvenile diabetes. Like so many kids with this disease she has adjusted amazingly well, the insulin pump she wears she's decorated hers with rhinestones. She can handle her own catheter needle. She's learned to sleep through the blood drawings in the wee hours of the morning. She's very brave. She is also quite right and understands full well the progress of her disease and what that might ultimately mean, blindness, amputation, diabetic coma. Every day she fights to have a future. What excuse will we offer this young woman should we fail her now? What might we tell her children or the millions of others who suffer that when given an opportunity to help we turned away, that facing political opposition we lost our nerve, that even though we knew better we did nothing. And should we fail, how will we feel if a few years from now a more enlightened generation should fulfill the promise of embryonic STEM cell therapy. Imagine what they would say of us who lacked the will. No, no, we owe this young woman and all those who suffer, we owe ourselves better than that. We are better than that. We are a wiser people, a finer nation and for all of us in this fight let me say we will prevail. The tide of history is with us. Like all generations who have come before ours, we are motivated by a thirst for knowledge and compelled to see others in need as fellow angels on an often difficult path deserving of our compassion. In a few months we will face a choice, yes, between two candidates and two parties but more than that. We have a chance to take a giant stride forward for the good of all humanity. We can choose between the future and the past, between reason and ignorance, between true compassion and mere ideology. This -- this is our moment and we must not falter. Whatever else you do come November 2 I urge you please cast a vote for embryonic STEM cell research. Thank you for your time. I am privileged to introduce to you a most extraordinary young woman, Ilana wexler is just 13 years old. But she long ago committed herself to a cause we all support, the defeat of George Bush and the election of John Kerry! Teresa Heinz Kerry, who shared the podium with her in San Francisco, was so impressed with Ilana that she invited her here tonight to be with us. Ladies and gentlemen, please join me in welcoming the founder of kids for Kerry, Ilana wexler.

speaker: Smith Whitaker
text: Hello. My name is Nubia Smith Whitaker. I'm 12 years old, and I'm from Framingham, Massachusetts. My friends and I are members of kids for Kerry. Why do we think John Kerry and John Edwards should be elected? Well, it's as easy as the Abc's. A is for a strong America that provides us hope, peace, and a great future. B is for better education. John Kerry knows that for American kids to be successful, we need the finest education. We need to have smaller classrooms and support our teachers. And c is for compassion for all children. In America, there are millions of kids that cannot go to the doctor when they're sick. But John Kerry wants to make sure that every child in our country gets the care they need. See? It's as simple as knowing your Abc's. Thank you for letting us kids speak to you tonight. We know our voices are heard in the Democratic party.

speaker: Teresa Heinz Kerry
text: Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. I love you too. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you, Christopher. Your father would be very proud of you and your brothers. And I love you and I love our family. My name is Teresa Heinz Kerry. And by now, I hope it will come as no surprise that I have something to say. Tonight, as I have done throughout this campaign, I would like to speak to you from the heart. And to all the Continental africans living in this country, and to all the new Americans in our country, I invite you to join in our conversation and together with us work towards the noblist purpose of all, a free, good, and Democratic society. I am grateful -- I am so grateful for the opportunity to stand before you and to say a few words about my husband, John Kerry. And why I firmly believe that he should be the next President of the United States. This is such a powerful moment for me. Like many other Americans, like many of you, and like even more, your parents and grandparents, I was not born in this country. And as you have seen, I grew up in East Africa, in mozambique, in a land that was then under a dictatorship. My father, a wonderful, caring man, who practiced medicine for 43 years, and who taught me how to understand disease and wellness, only got to vote for the first time when he was 73 years old. That's what happens in dictatorships. As a young woman, I attended a university in Johannesburg, South Africa, which was then not segregated. But I witnessed the weight of apartheid everywhere around me. And so, with my fellow students, we marched in the streets of Johannesburg against its extension into higher education. This was the late 1950's at the dawn of the civil rights marches in America. And as history records our efforts in South Africa failed and the higher education apartheid act passed, apartheid tightened its ugly grip, the shartsville riots followed and Nelson Mandela was arrested and sent to robin island. I learned something then, and I believe it still. There is a value in taking a stand whether or not anybody may be noticing it, and whether or not it is a risky thing to do. And if even those who are in danger can raise their lonely voices, isn't it more that is required of all of us in this land where liberty had her birth? I have a very personal feeling about how special America is. And I know how precious freedom is. It is a sacred gift sanctified by those who have lived it and those who have died defending it. My right to speak my mind, to have a voice, to be what some have called opinionated, is a right I deeply and profoundly cherish. And my only hope is that one day soon, women who have all earned their right to their opinions, instead of being called opinionated, will be called smart and well-informed, just like men. Tonight, I want to remember my mother's warmth, generosity, wisdom, and hopefulness and thank her for all the sacrifices she made on our behalf. Like so many other mothers, and this evening, I want to acknowledge and honor the women of this world whose wise voices for much too long have been excluded and discounted. It is time, it is time for the world to hear women's voices in full and at last. in the past year, I have been privileged to meet with Americans all across this land. They voiced many different concerns, but one they all share was about America's role in the world, what we want this great country of ours to stand for. To me, one of the best faces America has ever projected is the face of a peace corps volunteer. That face symbolizes this country, young, curious, brimming with idealism and hope, and a real honest compassion. Those young people convey an idea of America that is all about heart, creativity, generosity, and confidence, a practical can-do sense, and a big, big smile. For many generations of people, around this globe, that is what America has represented. A symbol of hope, a beacon brightly lit by the optimism of its people, people coming from all over the world. Americans believed that they can know all there is to know, build all there is to build, break down any barrier, tear down any wall. We sent men to the moon. And when that was not far enough, we sent galileo to jupiter. We sent cassini to sat turn. And hubble to touch the very edges of the universe. in the very dawn of time. Americans showed the world what can happen when people believe in amazing possibilities. And that, for me, is the spirit of America. The America you and I are working for in this election. It is the America that people across this nation want to restore from Iowa to California . From Florida to Michigan, and from Washington state to my home of Pennsylvania. It is the America the world wants to see, shining, hopeful, and bright once again. And that is the America that my husband, John Kerry, wants to lead. John believes in a bright future. He believes that we can and will invent the technologies, the new materials, and the conservation methods of the future. He believes that alternative fuels will guarantee that not only will no American boy or girl go to war because of our dependence on foreign oil, but also, that our economy will forever become independent of this need. We can, and we will, create good competitive and sustainable jobs while still protecting the air we breathe, the water we drink, and the health of our children. Because good environmental policy is good economics. John believes that we can and we will give every family and every child access to affordable healthcare, a good education, and the tools to become self-reliant. And John believes that we must and we should recognize the immense value of the caregivers in our country, those women and men who nurture and care for children, for elderly parents, for family members in need. These are the people who build and support our most valuable assets, our families. Isn't it time -- isn't it time that we began working to give parents more opportunity with their children, and wouldn't it be wonderful for parents to be able to afford a full and good family life? With John Kerry as president, we can and we will protect our nation's security without sacrificing our civil liberties. In short, John believes that we can and we must lead the world as America unique among nations, always should, by showing the face, not of its fears, but of our hopes. And John is a fighter. He earned his medals the old-fashioned way. By putting his life on the line for his country. And no one will defend this nation more vigorously than he will, and he will always, always be first in the line of fire. But he also knows the importance of getting it right. For him, the names of many friends inscribed on the vietnam memorial, that cold stone, testify to the awful toil exacted by leaders who mistake stubbornness for strength. And that is why, as president, my husband will not fear disagreement or dissent. He believes that our voices -- yours and mine -- must be the voices of freedom, and if we do not speak, neither does she. In America, the true patriots are those who use speak truth to power. And the truth that we must speak now is that America has responsibilities that it is time for us to accept again. With John Kerry as president, global climate change and other threats to the health of our planet will begin to be reversed. With John Kerry as president, the alliances that bind the community of nations and that truly make our country and the world a safer place, will be strengthened once more, and the Americans John and I have met in the course of this campaign all want America to provide hopeful leadership again. They want America to return to its moral bearings. And it is not -- it is not a moralistic America they seek. It is a moral nation that understands and willingly shareholders its obligations, a moral nation that rejects thoughtless and greedy choices in favor of thoughtful and generous actions. And it is a moral nation that leads to the power of its ideas and the power of its example. We can, and we should, join together to make the most of this great gift that we have all been given, this gift of freedom and this gift of America. In his first inaugural speaking to a nation on the eve of war, Abraham Lincoln said, "we must not be enemies. Though passion may have strained, it must not break our bonds of affection. The mystic courts of memory stretching from every battlefield and patriot grave to every living heart and hearthstone, all over this broad land, will yet swell the chorus of a union when again touched as surely there will be by the better angels of our nature. " today, the better angels of our nature are just waiting to be summoned. We only require a leader who's willing to call on them, a leader willing to draw again the mystic chords of our national memory and remind us of all that we, as a people, everyday leaders, can do. Of all that we, as a nation, stand for. And of all the immense possibility that still lies ahead. I think I've found that guy. And I'm married to him. John Kerry will give us back our faith in America. He will restore our faith in ourselves. And in the sense of limbless opportunity, that has always been America's gift to the world. Together, we will lift everyone up. We have to. It's possible. And you know what? It's the American thing to do. Good night, and God bless you.

speaker: Tom Carper
text: Thank you. Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. I'm going to talk with you today about making America stronger by eliminating our addiction to foreign oil. Last saturday, I filled up the gas tank on my minivan. It cost me about $35. That's 50% more than I paid at the same station just three years ago. The $35 isn't all that bothers me, though. I'm troubled by the fact that more than half the oil we use in America today is imported. I'm troubled by a trade deficit that will exceed a half trillion dollars this year. I'm troubled by the fact that about 1/5-that deficit comes from oil that flows from other places. But most of all I'm trouble bid the notion that some of my friends, some of the $35 will end up in the hands of people who will use that money to harm us and our people. Our President Is troubled by those things, too. He's had four years to do something about it. Well, I wish I could tell you that under his leadership, America has moved toward energy independence. The sad thing is, we're moving all right, we're moving backwards. Don't you believe that America can do better than this? With John Kerry as our president, we will. He will put America on the road to energy independence once again. And I've got to tell you, friends, it's about time. Now, let's talk about that road to energy independence and how to get moving on it. America is The Saudi Arabia of coal. We need to know how to turn coal into a gas that burns as cleanly as natural gas. All that's missing is the leadership and commitment. There are fields of soybeans growing across America from Delaware to Montana, the oils from those soybeans mixed with diesel fuel can cut our dependence on foreign oil and cut pollution too. Now, everyone is aware of the potential of solar energy, but I bet you didn't know this -- north dakota is The Saudi Arabia of wind. Harnessing that wind provides farmers with a new cash crop. It's called electricity. They sell it back to the local utilities and it's distributed throughout the region. Pollution goes down, farm income goes up. And speaking of new technology, that new technology will soon enable us to convert goren into ethanol more efficiently than anyone ever imagined. That ethanol will power the vehicles that we drive today and it will power the fuel cell vehicles that we drive tomorrow. Now, let me say a word about nuclear energy. You know, nuclear energy provides 20% of America's electricity today. 20%. A year ago, I took my son's boy scout troop to a nuclear power carrier with Teddy Roosevelt. It's 1,000 feet long, 25 stories high, deploys 75 aircraft and 5,000 sailors. Let me ask you a question -- how often do you think it stops to refuel? Once every 25 years. My friends, we have a choice. We can ignore that kind of potential or we could go to work to provide for safely disposing of nuclear waste. I say let's go to work. But we can't -- we can't become energy independent in America without also focusing on conservation. Did you know that we can move a ton of freight by rail from Boston to Washington on just one gallon of diesel fuel? That's right. One gallon. John Kerry knows that. And his transportation policies will reflect it. Did you know that the Clinton administration mandated the new air conditioners be more energy efficient? The current administration tried for years to kill that requirement. They failed. Manufacturers now say they can do it. What does all this mean to America? It means that 50 fewer power plants will be built in America, and it means that millions of tons of pollution won't end up in the air that we breathe every day. Now, wouldn't it be great to have a leader who actually fights for energy conservation instead of against it? Wouldn't it be great? None of what I talked about here is rocket science, either. It reflects good science. It's already been done and is waiting to be enlisted in a different kind of battle, a battle to make America energy independent. That struggle needs a new commander in chief. We need a President Who shares the sense of urgency that we feel here today. We need a leader who's already provided real leadership in this fight. In John Kerry, we will have that leader. And America will be stronger for it. Thank you very much. God bless. Thank you.

speaker: Tom Daschle
text: Thank you very much. Thank you. I want to thank you all and I thank the people of my home state of South Dakota for the honor of representing you in the United States Senate. Over the next few weeks, I will be driving from town to town you go to to south dakotans. One of my favorite things to do every August, I listen to farmers and ranchers and teachers, tribal leaders, police officers, small business owners, moms and dads, people who are struggling, and others who are doing well. They don't agree on every issue , but the common thread that binds them together is a love of our country. They understand that we have a moral responsibility to create a better future for our children and our grandchildren. And they want a stronger America here at home. South Dakotans reject the defeatist view that we have to have enough money to build Iraq but we can't afford to take care of the needs here at home. They wonder how we can build new schools in Iraq while so many American schools are crumbling? And it doesn't make sense to them that we're paying over $2 for gasoline at home, while American taxpayers are funding a nickel a gallon gas in Iraq. Americans aren't asking for special deals from Washington. They simply want their government to do right by America. Doing right by America means that we don't just talk about our values, we live them. And we honor the fundamental difference between right and wrong. John Kerry's entire life has been an inspirational example of doing right by America. When our generation was called to serve in Vietnam, he answered that call. He risked his life to save the life of others and let his small -- led his small band of brothers to safety. He didn't talk about patriotism. He lived it. When he came home -- when he came home from the war, he dedicated his life to public service. He worked first as a prosecutor, putting criminals behind bars, and as a united states Senator John Kerry has stood up to the drug companies and fought the lower prices for seniors. And he has continued to fight for his brothers in arms. I know. We fought side by side for veterans' health care, justices for victims of agent orange, and a full accounting of all Americans, p.o.w.'s and soldiers missing in action. We've done that. John Edwards -- John Edwards has spent his life fighting for the little guy. He's done his best to make things right when negligence caused terrible harm to children and families. And in the United States Senate, he has dedicated himself to making sure that American families are treated fairly by h.m.o. 's and insurance companies. John Kerry and John Edwards both understand that by doing right by America means accepting the moral responsibility of making America stronger and smarter and more secure. It means changing the way we do business in Washington. Today the middle class squeeze has become a way of life for millions of American families. Family incomes haven't risen in four years, but family expenses are soaring. Gas prices are up 22%. College tuition up 28%. Health care premiums up 36%. in the Kerry administration, the needs of regular families will be moved from the back burner to the front burner. Board room priorities won't crowd out the kitchen table values and the era of enron and Halliburton undermining our common purpose will come to an end. John Kerry and John Edwards and all Democrats will work to expand the circle of opportunity and prosperity for all Americans. We will fix America's tax code to reward companies keeping good-paying jobs here, not shipping them overseas. We will work -- we will work to lower the cost of health care. Every American should have the same health care options, the same price as members of Congress do. And we will work -- we will work to provide every senior with a real prescription drug benefit that actually lowers cost to seniors instead of handing billions of dollars to big drug companies and the h.m.o. 's. We're going to do that. We will invest in the new technologies that are the key to reducing America's dependence on imported oil, and we will make sure that our troops have the pay and the equipment and the health care they deserve and that our allies do their fair share to honor the commitment to America's veterans. Tonight, thousands of miles from here, brave men and women are serving in Iraq. Many are members of the national guard and the reserve from South Dakota and every state in our great nation. We owe them our profound thanks . But words alone aren't good enough. Thousands will return home with no health insurance because their jobs don't provide it. Or they simply can't afford it. We reject the claim that we can't afford to provide our troops with access to affordable health care. We reject that. And when our soldiers -- and when our soldiers do right by America, we must do right by them. America can do better, and we will with President John Kerry and Vice President John Edwards . And I pledge as the next majority leader of the United States, we will get America back on the right track. On November 2, Democrats will take back The United States Senate. We'll take back the Senate with Barack Obama and Brad Carson and all of our newly elected Democratic senators. We're going to do that. And with John Kerry and John Edwards and with our colleagues in the house and with all the support of hard-working, patriotic Americans across our great land, we will answer our country's call. We will do the right thing, and we will do right by America. Thank you, all, very, very much. Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: Al Sharpton
text: Thank you. Tonight I want to address my remarks in two parts. One, I'm honored to address the delegates here. Last friday I had the experience in Detroit of hearing President George Bush make a speech. And in the speech he asked certain questions. I hope he's watching tonight. I would like to answer your questions, mr. President. To our chairman, our delegates, and all that are assembled, we're honored and glad to be here tonight. I'm glad to be joined by supporters and friends from around the country. I'm glad to be joined by my family, kathy, dominique who will be 18, and Ashley. We are here, 2282 years, after right here in Boston we fought to establish the freedoms of America, the first President To die in the revolutionary war is buried not far from here, a black man from barbados named crispis adicts. 40 years ago in 1964, Fanny Lou Hama and the Mississippi Democratic family stood at the Democratic convention in Atlantic City, fighting to preserve voting rights for all America and all Democrats regardless of race or gender. Hamas stand inspired dr. King's March in selma which brought about the voting rights act of 1965. 20 years ago Reverend Jesse Jackson stood at the Democratic National Convention in San Francisco, again appealing to preserve those freedoms. Tonight we stand with knows freedoms at risk -- with those freedoms at risk and our security as citizens in question. I have come here tonight to say the only choice we have to preserve our freedom at this point in history is to Elect John Kerry, the President of the United States! I stood with both John Kerry and John Edwards on over 30 occasions, in debates during the primary season, and I not only debated them, I watched them. I observed their deeds. I looked into their eyes. I am convinced that they are men who say what they mean and mean what they say. I'm also convinced that at a time when a vicious spirit in the body of politics in this country that attempts to undermine America's freedoms, our civil rights, our civil liberties, we must lead this city and go forth and organize this nation for victory for our party and John Kerry and John Edwards in November! But let me quickly say, this is not just about winning an election. It's about preserving the principles on which this very nation was founded. Look at the current view of our nation worldwide as a result of our unilateral foreign policy. We went from unprecedented international support and solidarity on September 12, 2001, to hostility and hatred as we stand here tonight. We can't survive in the world by ourselves! How did we squander this opportunity to unite the world for democracy and to commit to the global fight against hunger and disease? We did it with a foreign policy based on flawed intelligence. We were told we were going to Iraq because there were weapons of mass destruction. We lost hundreds of soldiers. We spent $200 billion at a time when we had record state deficits. And when it became clear that there was no weapons, they changed the premise for the war and said, no, we went because of other reasons. If I told you tonight to let's leave the fleetcenter, we're in danger, and when you get outside you ask me, Reverend Al, what is the danger? And I say it don't matter, we just needed some fresh air, I have misled you and we were misled. We -- we are also faced with the prospect of, in the next four years, that two or more Supreme Court justices seats will become available. This year we celebrated the anniversary of brown vs. The board of education. This court has voted 5-4 on critical issues, of women's rights and civil rights. It is frightening to think that the gains of civil rights and those movements in the last century could be reversed if this administration is in the White House in these next four years. I suggest to you tonight that if George Bush had selected the court in 1954, clarence thomas would have never got to law school. This is not about a party. This is about living up to the promise of America. The proments of America says we will guarantee quality education for all children and not spend more money on metal detectors than computers in our schools. The promise of America guarantees health care for all of its citizens and doesn't force seniors to travel to Canada to buy prescription drugs they can't afford here at home. The promise of America provides that those who work in our health care system can afford to be hospitalized in the very beds they clean up every day. The promise of America is that government does not seek to regulate your behavior in the bedroom. But to guarantee your right to provide food in the kitchen. >> the goal of government is not to determine who may sleep together in the bedroom it is to help those who might not be eating in the kitchen. The promise of America is that we stand for human rights, whether it is fighting against slavery in the Sudan. Some are fasting around what is going on in Sudan. AIDS in lasuto or police misconduct in this country. The promise of America is one immigration promise whether they come from Mexico, Haiti, or Canada there must be one set of rules for everybody. We cannot welcome those to come and then try and act as though any culture will not be respected or treated inferior. We cannot look at the Latino community and preach one language. No one gave them an english test before they sent them to Iraq to fight for America. The promise of America is that every citizen's vote is counted and protected and election schemes do not decide the election. That, to me, is a glaring contradiction. We would fight for the right to vote for the people in the capital of Iraq in Baghdad but still don't give the federal right to vote for the people in the capital of the United States of America. Mr. President, as I close, mr. President, I heard you say friday that you had questions for voters. Particularly African-American voters. And you asked the question, did The Democratic Party take us for granted? Well, I have raised questions. Well, let me answer your question. You said The Republican Party was the party of Lincoln and Frederick Douglass. It is true that mr. Lincoln signed the emancipation proclamation after which there was a commitment to give 40 acres and the mule. This is the argument of reparations starts. We never got the 40 acres. We went all the way to herbert hoover and we never got the 40 acres. We didn't get the mule. So we decided we'd ride this donkey as far as it would take us. Mr. President, you said would we have more leverage if both parties got our votes. But we didn't come this far playing political games. It was those that earned our vote that got our vote. We got the civil rights act under Democrat. We got the voting rights act under Democrat. >> mr. President, the reason we are fighting so hard, the reason we took Florida so seriously is our right to vote wasn't gained because of our age. Our vote was soaked in the blood of martyrs. Soaked in the blood of Goodman and soaked in blood of those in Birmingham. This vote is sake red to us. This vote can't be -- sacred to us. This vote can't be bargained away. This vote can't be given away. Mr. President, in all due respect, mr. President, read my lips, our vote is not for sale. And as a whole generation of young leaders that have come forward across this country that stand on integrity and stand on their tradition. Those that have emerged with John Kerry and John Edwards as partners like greg meeks. Like maraka, like our voter registration director Martha Harris. Like those in the trenches. We come with strong family values. Family values is not squoust two car garages with a retirement plan. Retirement plans are good, but family values are also those who had to make nothing stretch into something happening, who had to make ends meet. I was raised by a single mother who made a way for me.
