speaker: Michelle Obama
text: As you might imagine, for Barack running for President Is nothing compared to that first game of basketball with my brother, Craig. I can't tell you how much it means to have Craig and my mom here tonight. Like Craig, I can feel my dad looking down owes, just as I felt his presence in every great moment of my life. And at 6'6", I've often felt like Craig was looking down on me, too, literally. But the truth is, both when we were kids and today, Craig wasn't looking down on me, he was watching over me. And he has been there for me every step of the way since that clear day in February 19 months ago when with little more than our faith in each other and a hunger for change, we joined my husband, Barack Obama, on the improbable journey that has led us to this moment. But each of us comes here also by way of our own improbable journey. I come here tonight as a sister blessed with a brother who is my mentor, my protector and my lifelong friend, and I come here as a wife who loves my husband and believes he will be an extraordinary president. And I come here as a mom, as a mom whose girls are the heart of my heart and the center of my world. They're the first things I think about when I wake up in the morning and the last thing I think about before I go to bed at night. Their future and all our children's future is my stake in this election. And I come here as a daughter, raised on the south side of Chicago by a father who was a blue collar city worker and a mother who stayed at home with my brother and me. My mother's love has always been a sustaining force for our family. And one of my greatest joys is seeing her integrity, her compassion, her intelligence reflected in my daughters. My dad was our rock, and although he was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis in his early 30's, he was our provider, he was our champion, our hero. But as he got sicker, it got harder for him to walk, took him longer to get dressed in the morning, you know. But if he was in pain, he never let on. He never stopped smiling and laughing, even while struggling to button his shirt, even while using two canes to get himself across the room to give my mom a kiss. He just woke up a little earlier and he worked a little harder. He and my mom poured everything they had into me and Craig. It was the greatest gift a child could receive, never doubting for a single minute that you're loved and cherished and have a place in this world. And changes to their faith and their hard work, we both were able to go to college. So I know first hand from their lives and mine that the American dream endures. And you know, what struck me when I first met ba ram, is even though he had this funny name and even though he had grown up all the way across the continent in Hawaii, his family was so much like mine. He was raised by grandparents who were working-class folks, just like my parents, and by a single mother who struggled to pay the bills, just like we did, and like my family, they scrimped and saved so that he could have opportunities that they never had for themselves. And Barack and I were raised with so many of the same values, like you work hard for what you want in life, that your word is your Bond, that you do what you say you're going to do, that you treat people -- -- with dignity and respect, even if you don't know them, and even if you don't agree with them. And ba ram and I set out to -- Barack and I set out to build our leist with those values and to pass them on to the next generation, because we want our children and all children in this nation to know that the only limit to the height of your achievement is the reach of your dreams and your willingness to work hard for them. And as our friendship grew and I learned more about Barack, he introduced me to the work that he had done when he first moved to Chicago after college. You see, instead of going to Wall Street, Barack went to work in the neighborhoods that had been devastated by the closing of steel plants. Jobs dried up. And Barack was invited back to speak to people from those neighborhoods about how to rebuild their community. And the people gathered there together that day were ordinary folks, doing the best they could to build a good life. See, they were parents trying to get by from paycheck to paycheck, grandparents trying to get it together on a fixed income, men frustrated that they couldn't support their families after a job that disappeared. You see, those folks weren't asking for a handout or a shortcut. See, they were ready to work. They wanted to contribute. They believed, like you and I believe that America should be a place where you can make it if you try. And Barack stood up that day and he spoke words that have stayed with me ever since. He talked about the world as it is and the world as it should be, and he said that all too often we accept the distance between the two and we settle for the world as it is, even when it doesn't reflect our values and as separations. -- and aspirations. But he reminded us that we also know what the world should look like. He said we know what fairness and justice and opportunity look like, and he urged us to believe in ourselves, to finds the strength within ourselves to strive for the world as it should be. And isn't that the great American story? It's the story of men and women gathered in churches and union halls and high school gyms and people who stood up and marched and risked everything they had, refusing to settle, determined to mole our future into the shape of our ideals. And it's because of their will and determination that this week we celebrate two anniversary, the 88th anniversary of women winning the right to vote -- -- and the 45th anniversary -- and the 45th anniversary of that hot summer day when dr. King lifted our sights and our hearts with his dream for our nation. And I stand here today at the cross-current of that history knowing that my piece of the American dream is a blessing hard won by those who came before me, all of them, driven by the same conviction that drove my dad to get up an hour early each day to painstakingly dress himself for work, the same conviction that drives the men and women I've met all across this country, people who worked the day shift then kiss their kids good night and head out for the night shift, without disappointment, without regret. See, that good night kiss is a reminder of everything they're working for. The military families who say grace each night with an empty seat at the table. The service men -- the service men and women who love this country so much they leave those they love most to defend it. The young people across America serving our communities, teaching children, cleaning up neighborhoods, caring for the least among us each and every day. People like Hillary Clinton. >> who put those 18 million cracks in that glass ceiling so that our daughters and our sons can dream a little bigger and aim a little higher. People like Joe Biden. Who has never forgotten where he came from and never stopped fighting for folks who worked long hours and faced long odds and need someone on their side again. All of us driven by the simple belief that the world as it is just won't do, that we have an obligation to fight for the world as it should be, and that is the thread that connects our hearts, that is the thread that runs through my journey and Barack's journey and so many other improbable journeys that have brought us here tonight, where the current of history meets this new tide of hope. And you see, that is why I love this country. >> and in my own life, in my own life, in my own small way, I've tried to give back to this country that has given me so much. See, that's why I left a job at a big law firm for a career in public service, working to empower young people to volunteer in their communities, because I believe that each of us, no matter what our age or background in life, each of us has something to contribute to the life of this nation. And it's a belief that Barack shares, a belief at the heart of his life's work. See, it's what he did all those years ago in Chicago, setting up job training to get people back to work and after-school programs to keep kids safe, working block by block to help people lift up their families. It's what he did in the illinois Senate, moving people from welfare to jobs, passing tax cuts for hard-working families and making sure women get equal pay for equal work. It's what he does in the United States Senate, fighting to ensure that men and women who serve this country are well combed home not just with medals and parades, but with good jobs and benefits and health care, including mental health care. To end the war responsibly, to build an economy that lift every family, to make sure health care is available for every American and to make sure that every single child in this nation has a world-class education all the way from preschool to college. That's what Barack Obama will do as President of the United States of America. He'll achieve these goals the same way he always has, by bringing us together and reminding us how much we share and how a life we really are. You see, Barack doesn't care where you're from or what your background is or what party you belong to. See, that's not how he sees the world. He knows that thread that connects us, our beliefs in America's promise, our commitment to our children's future, he knows that that thread is strong enough to hole us together as one nation, even when we disagree. It was strong enough to bring hope to those neighborhoods in Chicago, it was strong enough to pri hope to the mother he met who was worried about her child in Iraq, hope to the man who's unemployed and can't afford gas to find a job, hope to the student working nights to pay for his sister's health care, sleeping just a few hours a day, and it was strong enough to bring hope to people who came out on a cold iowa night and became the first voices in this chorus for change that have been echoed by millions of Americans from every corner of this nation, millions of Americans who know that Barack understands their dreams, millions of Americans who know that Barack will fight for people like them, and that Barack will bring finally the change that we need. And in the end -- and in the end, after all that's happened these past 19 months, see, the Barack Obama I know today is the same man I fell in love with 19 years ago. He's the same man who drove me and our new baby daughter home from the hospital 10 years ago this summer, inching along at a snail's pace, peering at us anxiously threw the rear-view mirror, feeling the whole weight of her future in his hands, determined to give her everything he had struggled so hard for himself, determined to give her something he never had -- the affirming embrace of a father's love. And as I took -- tuck that little girl in and her sister in the bed at night, you see, I think about how one day they'll have families of their own and how one day they and your sons and daughters will tell their own children about what we did together in this election. They'll tell them -- they'll tell them how this time we listened to our hopes instead of our fears, how this time -- how this time we decided to stop doubting and to start dreaming, how this time in this great country where a girl from the south side of Chicago can go to college and law school and the son of a single mother from Hawaii can go all the way to The White House. That we committed ourselves -- we committed ourselves to building the world as it should be. So tonight, in honor of my father's memory and my daughter's future, out of the gratitude for those whose triumphs we mark this week and those whose ever daye sacrifices have brought us to this moment, let us devote ourselves to finishing their work. Let us work together to fulfill their hopes and let's stands together to Elect Barack Obama, President of the United States of America. Thank you. God bless you, and God bless America.

With Barack Obama and Joe Biden in the White House, we will.

speaker: Eleanor Holmes Norton
text: Democrats, with the wind at our backs, we come to Denver as change-making, because ma-Biden Democrats. -- Obama-Biden Democrats. at the same time we embrace our nation's great unchanging principles as we seek to change the policies that have betrayed those principles. The nation's founders staked everything on creating a country where there would be no taxation without representation anywhere in America. In that tradition, Democrats proudly support the vote in Congress for the 600,000 citizens of our nation's capital. The district of columbia is not yet a state, but no one can doubt that the revolutionaries who invented America's most trusted national slogan did not create a new nation to get the vote only to turn around and deny the votes to the citizens of their own capital. Oh, no. 45 years ago this week, Martin Luther King jr. Inspired us to act on the principle that all Americans must have equal rights. Democrats will finish King's unfinished business for equal voting rights for the citizens of our capital. King's vision, that change is best achieved when wrapped in unchanging principles, is the hallmark of Democrats from Martin Luther King jr. To the next President of the United States, Barack Obama. The first residents of the district volunteered at the battles of Lexington and concord on the promise of the vote. Democrats will keep that promise. As yet again, district residents risk their lives in battles, in Iraq and Afghanistan. We will keep the promise made to the unknown soldier who was the first to die from the district in the war against taxation without representation and we will keep that promise for 21-year-old d.c. National guard specialist darrell dent, the first d.c. Resident to die for his country in Iraq. The new Democratic Congress already has swiftly led the nation to resolve our nation's oldest unare resolved human rights issue. We thank The Democratic House of Representatives for passing the d.c. Voting rights act. The Senate -- the Senate came within three votes. Tonight, we challenge the Senate, especially the Republicans, to match the house this session and pass the bill. Then have no doubt, fellow Democrats, if George Bush won't sign the d.c. Voting rights act, its most prominent co-sponsor urk our next president, Barack Obama, will.

speaker: Federico F. Pena
text: Welcome to Denver. Bienvenidos a Denver. Welcome to our beautiful city in this color wrote -- call will stay in this most incredible country on earth. We in Colorado are proud of our state. Along the way, we had our share of tough times. When I became Mayor Of Denver in 1983, everyone knew we had a serious problem with our air pollution. On a bad day you could barely see our magnificent mountains. Some people said we could not fix it. Our economy was in a recession. We were struggling with the shock of an energy crisis. Does that sound familiar? But we knew we could not afford more of the same and we made a decision to change. We started using cleaner fuels. We invested in mass transit. Everyone chipped in and did their part. A funny thing happened. Our city that cleaner. And our economy started to grow. We took our future into our own hands and build a new airport for the new century. Today, new energy companies are coming to Colorado and building wind turbines and plants that are creating more and more engineering and manufacturing jobs that pay good wages. But we meet here today in the shadow of a greater challenge. A challenge that goes beyond Colorado. Today our nation faces yet another energy crisis. Every day, Americans all across our land are wondering how they can afford to pay for the gasoline they need to get to their jobs. How the can afford to pay their light bills. Their heating and cooling bills. They wonder if there will be anything left for a college education for medical emergencies or for retirement. They wonder how did this happen? Our addiction to oil, our dependence on imports, and our greenhouse gas pollution are all getting worse every year. America is on a liquid leash. Elease that is choking our paychecks and the prosperity of our nation. For eight years now, President Bush and Dick Cheney have rubberstamp and energy policy written by big oil companies and their lobbyists in Washington. What does John McCain want to do now? The simple things that keep on failing. Making record profits and more of the same roadblocks in front of fuel-efficient vehicles. More of the refusal to support renewable energy. Americans who had to pay $4 a gallon per-the summer know we cannot afford more of the same. Barack Obama will bring a change we need. To reduce our dependence on foreign oil, to increase our use of home grown clean fuels to create 5 million new green power jobs here in America. He will invest 100 tricky billion dollars over 10 years to build new energy technologies here at home and we can export them all around the world. He will raise mileage standards so our cars can go farther on less gas. He will help American industry retooled to build a car for the future in and give tax credits to Americans to buy them. These are not just dreams. This is America's future. Taking back our own energy destiny. Putting the best minds of our great universities and research centers to work just as we did when John Kennedy, a Democratic president, committed this to putting a man on the moon. American energy, American technology, American jobs, ready to be created right now. That is the change we need. And to those who doubt that we can solve our energy problems and create new American jobs and economic growth at the same time, I say come to Denver. And come to states like Pennsylvania and New Mexico and Colorado and New Jersey and Montana. All being transformed by Democratic governors building new green economies. And so tonight, I ask each of you as well as Americans across our great land to believe. That we can win ourselves of foreign oil. We can create millions of jobs in green industries. We can develop our own energy sources with Barack Obama as our president. The President Who has challenged us to believe in ourselves, we will get the change we need. Will transform our future and power our country for generations to come. Thank you all very much.

speaker: Gloria Craven
text: Hello, everybody. In from North Carolina. My story is not different rollout of other acts in this country. I live in a town that was built on textiles. Most of us did not have much growing up. speaker: Hillary Clinton
text: Thank you. Thank you all very very much. Thank you all very much. I am honored to be here tonight. A proud mother. A proud Democrat. A proud American. And a proud supporter of Barack Obama. My friends, it is time to take back the country we love. Whether you voted for me, or voted for Barack, the time is now to unite as a single party with a single purpose. We are on the same team, and none of us can sit on the sidelines. This is a fight for the future. And it's a fight we must win. I haven't spent the past 35 years in the trenches advocating for children, campaigning for universal health care, helping parents balance work and family, and fighting for women's rights at home and around the world . . . To see another Republican in the White House squander the promise of our country and the hopes of our people. And you haven't worked so hard over the last 18 months, or endured the last eight years, to suffer through more failed leadership. No way. No how. No McCain. Barack Obama is my candidate. And he must be our president. Tonight we need to remember what a presidential election is really about. When the polls have closed, and the ads are finally off the air, it comes down to you — the American people, your lives, and your children's futures. For me, it's been a privilege to meet you in your homes, your workplaces, and your communities. Your stories reminded me everyday that America's greatness is bound up in the lives of the American people — your hard work, your devotion to duty, your love for your children, and your determination to keep going, often in the face of enormous obstacles. You taught me so much, you made me laugh, and . . . You even made me cry. You allowed me to become part of your lives. And you became part of mine. I will always remember the single mom who had adopted two kids with autism, didn't have health insurance and discovered she had cancer. But she greeted me with her bald head painted with my name on it and asked me to fight for health care. I will always remember the young man in a marine corps t-shirt who waited months for medical care and said to me: "take care of my buddies; a lot of them are still over there....and then will you please help take care of me?" I will always remember the boy who told me his mom worked for the minimum wage and that her employer had cut her hours. He said he just didn't know what his family was going to do. I will always be grateful to everyone from all fifty states, Puerto Rico and the territories, who joined our campaign on behalf of all those people left out and left behind by the Bush administration. To my supporters, my champions — my sisterhood of the traveling pantsuits – from the bottom of my heart: thank you. You never gave in. You never gave up. And together we made history. Along the way, America lost two great Democratic champions who would have been here with us tonight. One of our finest young leaders, Arkansas Democratic Party chair, Bill Gwatney, who believed with all his heart that America and the south could be and should be Democratic from top to bottom. And Congresswoman Stephanie tubbs jones, a dear friend to many of us, a loving mother and courageous leader who never gave up her quest to make America fairer and smarter, stronger and better. Steadfast in her beliefs, a fighter of uncommon grace, she was an inspiration to me and to us all. Our heart goes out to Stephanie's son, mervyn, jr, and bill's wife, rebecca, who traveled to Denver to join us at our convention. Bill and Stephanie knew that after eight years of George Bush, people are hurting at home, and our standing has eroded around the world. We have a lot of work ahead. Jobs lost, houses gone, falling wages, rising prices. The Supreme Court in a right-wing headlock and our government in partisan gridlock. The biggest deficit in our nation's history. Money borrowed from the Chinese to buy oil from the saudis. Putin and Georgia, Iraq and Iran. I ran for President To renew the promise of America. To rebuild the middle class and sustain the American dream, to provide the opportunity to work hard and have that work rewarded, to save for college, a home and retirement, to afford the gas and groceries and still have a little left over each month. To promote a clean energy economy that will create millions of green collar jobs. To create a health care system that is universal, high quality, and affordable so that parents no longer have to choose between care for themselves or their children or be stuck in dead end jobs simply to keep their insurance. To create a world class education system and make college affordable again. To fight for an America defined by deep and meaningful equality - from civil rights to labor rights, from women's rights to gay rights, from ending discrimination to promoting unionization to providing help for the most important job there is: caring for our families. To help every child live up to his or her God-given potential. To make America once again a nation of immigrants and a nation of laws. To bring fiscal sanity back to Washington and make our government an instrument of the public good, not of private plunder. To restore America's standing in the world, to end the war in Iraq, bring our troops home and honor their service by caring for our veterans. And to join with our allies to confront our shared challenges, from poverty and genocide to terrorism and global warming. Most of all, I ran to stand up for all those who have been invisible to their government for eight long years. Those are the reasons I ran for president. Those are the reasons I support Barack Obama. And those are the reasons you should too. I want you to ask yourselves: were you in this campaign just for me? Or were you in it for that young marine and others like him? Were you in it for that mom struggling with cancer while raising her kids? Were you in it for that boy and his mom surviving on the minimum wage? Were you in it for all the people in this country who feel invisible? We need leaders once again who can tap into that special blend of American confidence and optimism that has enabled generations before us to meet our toughest challenges. Leaders who can help us show ourselves and the world that with our ingenuity, creativity, and innovative spirit, there are no limits to what is possible in America. This won't be easy. Progress never is. But it will be impossible if we don't fight to put a Democrat in the White House. We need to Elect Barack Obama because we need a President Who understands that America can't compete in a global economy by padding the pockets of energy speculators, while ignoring the workers whose jobs have been shipped overseas. We need a President Who understands that we can't solve the problems of global warming by giving windfall profits to the oil companies while ignoring opportunities to invest in new technologies that will build a green economy. We need a President Who understands that the genius of America has always depended on the strength and vitality of the middle class. Barack Obama began his career fighting for workers displaced by the global economy. He built his campaign on a fundamental belief that change in this country must start from the ground up, not the top down. He knows government must be about "we the people" not "we the favored few." and when Barack Obama is in the White House, he'll revitalize our economy, defend the working people of America, and meet the global challenges of our time. Democrats know how to do this. As I recall, President Clinton and the Democrats did it before. And President Obama and the Democrats will do it again. He'll transform our energy agenda by creating millions of green jobs and building a new, clean energy future. He'll make sure that middle class families get the tax relief they deserve. And I can't wait to watch Barack Obama sign a health care plan into law that covers every single American. Barack Obama will end the war in Iraq responsibly and bring our troops home – a first step to repairing our alliances around the world. And he will have with him a terrific partner in Michelle Obama. Anyone who saw Michelle's speech last night knows she will be a great first lady for America. Americans are also fortunate that Joe Biden will be at Barack Obama's side. He is a strong leader and a good man. He understands both the economic stresses here at home and the strategic challenges abroad. He is pragmatic, tough, and wise. And, of course, Joe will be supported by his wonderful wife, Jill. They will be a great team for our country. Now, John McCain is my colleague and my friend. He has served our country with honor and courage. But we don't need four more years . . . Of the last eight years. More economic stagnation ...and less affordable health care. More high gas prices ...and less alternative energy. More jobs getting shipped overseas ...and fewer jobs created here. More skyrocketing debt ...home foreclosures ...and mounting bills that are crushing our middle class families. More war . . . Less diplomacy. More of a government where the privileged come first ...and everyone else comes last. John McCain says the economy is fundamentally sound. John McCain doesn't think that 47 million people without health insurance is a crisis. John McCain wants to privatize social security. And in 2008, he still thinks it's okay when women don't earn equal pay for equal work. With an agenda like that, it makes sense that George Bush and John McCain will be together next week in the twin cities. Because these days they're awfully hard to tell apart. America is still around after 232 years because we have risen to the challenge of every new time, changing to be faithful to our values of equal opportunity for all and the common good. And I know what that can mean for every man, woman, and child in America. I'm a united states Senator Because in 1848 a group of courageous women and a few brave men gathered in seneca falls, New York, many traveling for days and nights, to participate in the first convention on women's rights in our history. And so dawned a struggle for the right to vote that would last 72 years, handed down by mother to daughter to granddaughter – and a few sons and grandsons along the way. These women and men looked into their daughters' eyes, imagined a fairer and freer world, and found the strength to fight. To rally and picket. To endure ridicule and harassment. To brave violence and jail. And after so many decades – 88 years ago on this very day – the 19th amendment guaranteeing women the right to vote would be forever enshrined in our constitution. My mother was born before women could vote. But in this election my daughter got to vote for her mother for president. This is the story of America. Of women and men who defy the odds and never give up. How do we give this country back to them? By following the example of a brave new yorker , a woman who risked her life to shepherd slaves along the underground railroad. And on that path to freedom, Harriett Tubman had one piece of advice. If you hear the dogs, keep going. If you see the torches in the woods, keep going. If they're shouting after you, keep going. Don't ever stop. Keep going. If you want a taste of freedom, keep going. Even in the darkest of moments, ordinary Americans have found the faith to keep going. I've seen it in you. I've seen it in our teachers and firefighters, nurses and police officers, small business owners and union workers, the men and women of our military – you always keep going. We are Americans. We're not big on quitting. But remember, before we can keep going, we have to get going by electing Barack Obama president. We don't have a moment to lose or a vote to spare. Nothing less than the fate of our nation and the future of our children hang in the balance. I want you to think about your children and grandchildren come election day. And think about the choices your parents and grandparents made that had such a big impact on your life and on the life of our nation. We've got to ensure that the choice we make in this election honors the sacrifices of all who came before us, and will fill the lives of our children with possibility and hope. That is our duty, to build that bright future, and to teach our children that in America there is no chasm too deep, no barrier too great – and no ceiling too high – for all who work hard, never back down, always keep going, have faith in God, in our country, and in each other. Thank you so much. God bless America and godspeed to you all.

I know all of you will work hard, and together we will Elect A President Does the good as the people of our past great country. Thank you, everyone to read it on, Wisconsin, and God bless America. Thank you.

speaker: Jim Whitaker
text: Good evening. We are Americans first and we're not Democrats and we're not Republicans, and I stand before you as a father and as a husband, as an air and as a proud Alaskan but first and foremost I stand before you as an American. I am not here to speak against John McCain. I am a registered Republican. I am here to endorse Barack Obama for the presidency of the United States of America. I do that because I'm an American first. As an American, each of us as a precious vote in the responsibility to cast that vote for the candidate that we believe will best meet America's challenges and seize the opportunities for America. I will be casting mind for the candidate who possesses the keen intellect and pragmatic idealism and a realistic and resulting wisdom which is sorely needed in America today. I will be casting my vote for Barack Obama. We will get some judgment for Barack Obama. We Americans had must have -- we must have American policies on what is best for hard working American families, not special interests, not lobbyists, not just for the very wealthy, but the vast majority of our people. We must have an energy policy that leads us to energy independence and stability and we will get that from Barack Obama. This cornerstone of our economic security and national security cannot be left to the winds of the marketplace which are subject to shifts and whims of hostile dictators. America's energy policy must be based on self-reliance and the resources we have right here at home from domestic protest ackman's, including liquefied natural gas from Alaska to the wind into the solar. Renewable alternative energy sources, American energy sources. in the end, I am here because I know the change we need requires a leader with steely resolve, and a powerful vision for our future and the passion for helping the working people. speaker: Joe Manchin
text: I am the Governor Of the great state of West Virginia. And Chairman Of The Democratic Governors Association. It is an honors to stand before you today. Governors work across party lines to get things done. We are held accountable each and every day for finding ways to solve our state's most pressing problems. 28 states including red and blue states currently have Democrats in the Governor Office. They are doing what hard-working families have to do, living within their means, keeping their budgets in balance. Our goal is to simply keep our country strong and our economy is vibrant, state-by-state. West Virginia state leadership has produced a record number of new jobs. Low unemployment, and fewer taxes to read -- and your taxes. We reduced the size of state government and tackle our debts. Now the time has done -- come for Washington to follow our example and bridge partisan divides to bring America the changing needs three. The states of our union cannot afford any more of the same old gridlock that currently operates in Washington. What we need from Washington is a realistic energy policy as well as health care for every working person. We need a commitment to fixing are roads and bridges and a renewed education system that prepares our work force to compete globally. Together we can turn the page on November the fourth by voting for the one candidate who can bring us the change we need. Barack Obama. I was not born with a silver spoon in my mouth, but I would like to say I am a child of privilege. My grandparents were Italians and checks the walk-in immigrants to search for a better life, and went to the small coal mining towns of West Virginia. They understood that life in America is a privilege. In exchange for the privilege they believed they had a responsibility to give something back. They taught me the importance not only of hard work but also of having compassion for all people. The example they set along with the unconditional love of my family and the strong support of my community enabled me to attend college, run a successful business, and launch a career in public service. I am very proud to represent the great state of West Virginia, and most importantly it's people. West Virginians value hardware, patriotism, and faith in God they have faced adversity with courage and they help each other in challenging times. They do the heavy lifting in the economy. A month nickel, make the steel, and work the assembly lines that make our energy -- industries stake. Their experience and knowledge has earned each of them a ph.d. In life. They can shake your hand, look you in the eye and touch your heart. And let me tell you something. They cannot afford four more years to life the last eight. They need and deserve better from Washington. They need a President Who puts their best interests first. They need a President Who will tackle the major issues, balance our nation's budget and help struggling middle class families regain their footing. They need a President Who will re-establish our country's reputation in the world community. They need a President Who will restore the promise of economic opportunity that is every American's right. They need to change, and a Barack Obama as president. We can move from recession to resurgence. Together we can stop running from our domestic problems and start spreading towards solutions. We can raise our expectations of Washington and demand that they tackle the tough challenges just as our nation's governors to every day. My fellow Americans, we need a new leader for a new time. Barack Obama is the only candidate in this race who has captured our nation's hopes for the kind of change we so desperately need. Let's do what is right for America and select Barack Obama as the next President of the United States of America. Thank you. God bless you. God save the state of West Virginia. And may God continue to bless the united states of America. Thank you.

speaker: John Chiang
text: Good evening. Four years ago this son of the Kansas father stood before you and he lifted the hearts and hopes and reminded us that we can achieve great is. As I watched him speak, but could not help that think that it was my story. It is our story. It is the American story. On the surface, it could be viewed that Barack Obama to copyrights have little in common with my parents. His came from africa and mine came from asia and they spoke different languages. Our parents had much in common because they were indicted by the values that they taught their sons. In America, it does not matter where you come from, but where you are going. It does not matter what your name is, but whether you are willing to work hard and make a name for yourself. Barack Obama's life reaffirms the American dream. There are countless American families to the east shores to rocky mountains and to California. This is our nation's greatest promise, but the promise grows harder to reach. As the comptroller of the great state of California, our responsibility is to build a broader fiscal future for my state and for their 37 million residents. California is struggling with for closures and record as prices and rising unemployment. We are working harder than ever, but many of us are falling further and further behind. We know it is still golden and we know that path to the promised land and the Barack Obama will lead us there. He understands as John F. Kennedy said, that a rising tide lifts all boats. Together we will rise. Together we will fall. Here in Denver and all across the great country, we choose to rise. Together we will rise. We will reject the last four years of policy. Together, we will rise above the common challenges of poverty and access to health care and energy independence. Barack Obama will give us the change we need so that the values of hard working people what our parents taught us were just as loud and true for our children as they did for us. Once again, let's build a country that embraces the greatest promise of all for people. Thank you and God bless you all.

speaker: John Conyers
text: Brothers and sisters, I knew Stephanie as a constant voice for justice and peace. speaker: Katherine Marcano
text: Thank you very much. In here today because I dare to believe and to hope. It is not easy growing up in poverty and going to bed hungry are going to school in inappropriate shoes. speaker: Kathleen Sebelius
text: Good evening. A shout out to the sunflower state. Good evening. I'm Kathleen Sebelius. Governor Of the great state of Kansas. Tonight is the night to reflect on America's promise of a change we need to keep it alive for generations to come. Just as it was kept alive for all of us. I am a descendant of Irish immigrants. My great-grandmother worked as a maid in the home of President William Howard taft. Decades later, the president's grandson and my father the grandson of the made serve back- to-back to represent the same district in Congress. That is the American dream. It is my story and the story of millions of others. Last night we heard Barack Obama's story. How the son of a single mother from Kansas through hard work and perseverance has come within reach of The White House. Barack Obama was raised by a family of pragmatic, hard- working kansans who believe in fate and -- faith and family and community. But believes in a country club economics and once more tax cuts for dick -- big corporations will giving a third of the middle class tax relief that Barack Obama will deliver pre Barack Obama knows it is time to help the people who focus on hedgerows more than hedge funds. Not John McCain. He supports tax breaks for companies that move overseas brick Barack Ramallah wants to give tax breaks to companies to create jobs here home. Barack Obama knows that after a life of hard work especially after years of working the land or factories, you deserve to retire in security. He will cut taxes for seniors who make less than $50,000 a year. He will make it easier for every worker to save and build a nest egg. Barack Obama will strengthen social security but not John McCain. He wants to privatize social security putting at risk your retirement and future. John McCain promises more of the same. Barack Obama will deliver the change we need. We need fuel for heartland can be found in cropp that grow in our fertile Perry's. The energy for tomorrow is there and the clean grenoble of the steady -- power of the steady winds. Investing in American energy will create 5 million new green jobs. Jobs that will revitalize many of our communities. Jobs that can never be outsourced to a foreign supplier. Now even though John McCain has spent 26 years in Washington, voting over and over again against investing in renewable energy, John McCain does support summer nobles. He wants to renew the failed Bush agenda for another four years. John McCain has renewed the Bush-rove style of politics. Built on bringing down your opponent instead of lifting of America. As a Governor Who works with the Republican legislation every day, I can tell you we cannot bring about positive change unless we fix are divisive politics. We need to heed the words of another President Who came out of Illinois at a difficult time to lead this country. A man who warned us that a house divided against itself cannot stand. With Barack Obama as our president, together we will turn the page on the politics of the past. We will create opportunities that once again will make this country a place where the children of presidents and the children of maids share common destiny. Where once again we can reach through difficulties and touched the stars. That is the kind of change America needs. Thank you.

speaker: Lilly Ledbetter
text: Good evening. Good evening. Many of you are probably asking, who was that grandmother from Alabama at the podium? I can assure you nobody is more surprised or humbled then I am. I am here to talk about America's commitment to fairness and equality and how people like me and people like you suffer when the commitment is betrayed. How fitting that I speak to you on women's equality day when we celebrate -- when we celebrate ratification of the amendment that gave the women the right to vote. Even as we celebrate, let's also remind ourselves that the fight for equality is not over. And I know that from personal experience. I was a trailblazer when I went to work as a female supervisor at a goodyear tire plant in Alabama. My job demanded a lot and I give it 100%. I kept up with every one of my male co-workers. Toward the end, of my 19 years at goodyear, I began to suspect that I was not getting paid as much as the man doing the same job. An anonymous note in my mailbox confirmed that I was right. Despite raising me for my work, goodyear gave me smaller raises than my male co-workers over and over. Those differences affected my family's quality of life then and they affect my retirement now. What I discovered -- when I discovered the injustice I thought about moving on but in the end, I could not ignore the discrimination. So I went to court. A jury -- a jury agreed with me. They found that my employer had violated a law. And awarded me what I was owed. I hoped the verdict would make my company feel the sting, learn a lesson, and never again treat women unfairly. But the appeal. -- they appealed. All the way to The Supreme Court. In a five-four decision our highest court sided with big business. They said I should have filed my complete within six months of good years first decision to pay less even though I had no idea that I was being paid less. And what they were doing was wrong. In dissent justice Ruth B. Ginsberg wrote that the ruling does not make sense in the real world and she was right. The house of representatives passed a bill to make sure that what was done to me could not happen again. When I got to the Senate, Republicans opposed it to prevent a vote. We cannot afford more of the same votes that deny women their equal rights. Barack Obama is on our side. He is fighting for -- to fix this terrible ruling and as president, he has promised to appoint justices who will enforce laws that protect everyday people like me. This is not a Democratic or Republican issue. It is a fairness issue. There are some Republicans and a lot of Democrats who are on our side. My case is over. I will never receive the pay I deserve. There will be a far reaching reward if we secure their pay for our children and our grandchildren. So that no one will ever have to suffer an experience that I did. Equal pay for equal work is a fundamental -- equal pay for equal work is a fundamental American principle. We need leaders in this country who will fight for you. And with all of us working together, we can have the change we need and the opportunity we all deserve. Thank you.

speaker: Linda T. Sanchez
text: Good afternoon. I'm Congresswoman Linda Sanchez and I'd like to share a story with you. Not too long ago, when I was walking through the capitol on my way to vote, I had to throw my arm into the closing doors of an elevator to catch it. When I stepped in, there were two men there and one turned to me and said, so whose office do you work in? Well, dressed professionally and wearing my pin that identifies me as a member of Congress, I was a little dismayed these two automatically assumed I was somebody else's staff. I politely responded oh, I have my own office in this building. The doors to the elevators opened and the two of them scurried out and not another word was said. As a young Latina, most people may not think of me as a person they would go to first in search of answers, but I'll tell you somebody who did. Barack Obama. He recognized that my background as a labor union member and organizer gave me a grasp of the difficulty that families encounter in trying to make ends meet. That's why he picks up the phone to ask me about policies that impact working families. With the soaring cost of energy, families today are paying more for basics like food and fuel and just about everything else. Rising health care costs mean that for many people, coverage is out of reach. With the joblessness rate on the rise, hard working families are being squeezed from all sides. They worry about their economic security and whether tomorrow will be more -- bring more hardship than happiness. But Barack Obama knows what we need to get the American economy back on track. He's restoring America's promise that if you work hard and you play by the rules, you won't be left behind. Barack Obama understands what single parent families confront every single day because he grew up in a single parent home. Barack Obama understands that we must preserve programs like low interest student loans because that's how he was able to afford to go to college. Barack Obama understands that as Americans, we can achieve economic success, but also lend a hand to help those who are struggling. Because he did just that when he became an advocate and an organizer for communities that had been devastated by plant closures. There are people listening to me tonight who think that the outcome of this election really doesn't affect them. But it will. It will determine whether or not you have a champion on your side that's going to help you get into that elevator, or whether we're going to have four more years of failed leadership that's given hard-working families the shaft. Thank you so much and God bless.

speaker: Mark R. Warner
text: Thank you. Are you ready? Hello, Virginia. My fellow Democrats. I fellow Americans. -- my fellow Americans. The most important contest of our generation has begun. Now the campaign for the presidency. Not the campaign for Congress. But the race for the future. And I believe from the bottom of my heart with the right vision, and the right leadership, and the energy and creativity of the American people, there is no nation that we cannot outhust le and outcompete and no American need be left behind. Yes. The race for the future is on. It will not be won if only some Americans are in the running. It will not be won with yesterday's ideas. And yesterday's editions -- divisions and it will not be won with a President Who was stuck in the past. We need a President Who understands the world today. The future we seek, and the change we need. We need Barack Obama as the next President of the United States. Now I have a unique perspective. Like many of you, I am the first in my family to graduate from college. It was made possible by supportive parents, great public schools, and since my folks did not have the resources, thank goodness for the student loan program. After a graduated law school, -- after I graduated law school, it did not take me long to realize that America would not miss me as a lawyer. I started a business. My first company failed in six weeks. My next one was much more successful. It failed in six months. And then a buddy of mine told me about this brand new idea. This thing called car telephones. Cell phones. Friends of mine told me you are crazy. Go get a real job. Nobody is ever going to want a phone in their car. But I saw a different future. And with luck and a lot of hard work, I got in on the ground floor of the cell phone industry. There's only one country in the world where I could have received an education, where I have been given not just one chance or two but three and rex could have succeeded and that is this country. The united states America. -- united states of America. At our best, it is not your lineage or last name that matters. It is not where you come from. It is where you want to go. In America, everyone should get a fair shot. Barack Obama understands this. He has lived it. And Barack Obama is running to restore that fair shot for every American. But when we look around we see that for far too many Americans, that fair shot is becoming more of a long shot. How many kids have the grades to go to college but not the money? How many families always thought their home would be their safest investment? How many of our soldiers coming back after their second or third tour of duty wondering if the education and health care benefits that were promised would actually be there? Two wars, a warming planet, and an energy policy that says let's go borrow money from China so we can buy oil from countries that do not like this. How many people do not -- look at these things and wonder what is the future holding for them? And their children? And their family? Their country? How many in George Bush and John McCain's America? Far too many. Let's be fair. Some of these changes were inevitable. But all of them are more severe, more immediate, and more threatening because of the misguided policies and outdated thinking of this administration. Books always ask me, what is my biggest criticism of President Bush? I am sure you have got your own. But here is mine. It is not just the policy differences. It is the fact that this President Never tapped into our greatest resource. The character and resolve of the American people. He never really asked us to step up. Think about it. If after September 11, there had been a call from the President To get ourselves of foreign oil so we would no longer be funding the terrorists who just attacked us, every American would have said, how can I do my part? This administration failed to lead in what we can achieve as a nation when all this work together. John McCain promises more of the same period a plan that would explode the deficit and leave it to our kids. No plan to invest in our infrastructure and continue spending $10 billion a month in Iraq. I do not know about you. That is just not right. That is four more years that we cannot afford. Barack Obama has a different vision and a different plan. Right now at this critical moment in our history we have one shot to get it right. And the status quo will not cut it. Let me tell you, if you think you have seen dramatic changes in the world in technology in the last 10 years, you ain't seen nothing yet. The races on. If you watch the live picture now China is going for the goal. -- America has never been afraid of the future and we should not start now. If we choose the right path, if we choose the right path, every one of these challenges is also an opportunity. Look at energy. If we got ourselves of foreign oil which could make our country safer. We could solve global warming and with the right policies, within 24 months, we will be building a 100 mile per gallon plug in vehicles right here with American technology and American workers. Look at health care. If we bring down costs and cover everyone, not only will America be healthier, we will be more competitive in the global economy. Just think about this. In four months, we will have an administration that believes in science. We can again lead the world in life-saving and life changing cures. Think about education. If we recruit a new army of teachers and give our schools the resources to meet our highest standards, not only will every child in America be given a fair shot, but the American economy will be given a shot in the arm. Whether they want to be an engineer or an electrician, every kid will be trained for the jobs of the 21st century. Or look at America's standing in the world. If we rebuild our military and rebuild our alliances, we can rally the world to defeat terrorism and restore America's leadership. Which candidate understands these opportunities? Which candidate knows that we do not have another four years to waste? Barack Obama. Barack Obama also knows this as well. We need leaders. Who see our common ground as sacred ground. We need leaders who will appeal to us not as Republicans or Democrats. But first and foremost as Americans. I spent 20 years in business and if you ran a company whose only strategy was to tear down the competition, it would not last very long. So why is this wisdom so hard to find in Washington? I know we are at the Democratic convention. If an idea works, it really does not matter. Whether it has a d or an r next to it. This election is not about liberal against conservative or left against right. It is about the future vs. The past. In this election at this moment, at this moment in our history, we know what the problems are. We know what this critical -- at this critical juncture we have one shot to get it right and we know these new times demand new thinking. We believe in success. We believe that everyone should have an opportunity to get ahead. And with that success comes a responsibility to make sure that others can follow. I think we're blessed to be Americans. With that blessing comes an obligation to our neighbors and to our common good. You give every child tools they need to succeed. That means quality schools, access to health care, safe neighborhoods, not to it -- not just because it is the right thing to do. Of course it is. If those kids do better, we all do better. It does not really matter. You can be softhearted or hardheaded. Both are going to lead you to the same place. We're all in this together. That is what this party believes. That is what this nation believes. That is what Barack Obama and Joe Biden believe. We can do it. If we can do it. Sure we can. When I became governor, this is what Virginia face. A massive budget shortfall. An economy that was not moving. The gridlock in the capital. Does that sound familiar? What do we do? Working together, the Democratic Governor With the two to one Republican legislature and a lot of good folks who did not see themselves as Republicans or Democrats but Virginians. We close the budget gap and Virginia was named the best managed in the nation. We made record investments in education and job training. We got 98% of all eligible kids enrolled in our children's health care program. We delivered broadband to the most remote areas of our state. Because in this global economy, if you can send a job to bangalore, India, you sure can send one to Danville, Virginia and Flint, Michigan. And Scranton, Pennsylvania. And Peoria, Illinois. in the global economy, he should not have to leave your home town to find a world class job. Let me tell you. About a place called Lebanon. Lebanon, Virginia. It is in the coal fields of southwest Virginia. That whole town, the population of the whole town could fit right here on the convention floor. Lebanon is like a lot of small towns in America. It has seen the industries that sustain it downsized, outsourced, or shut down. Some folks look at towns like Lebanon and say tot-lot in the global economy you have lost. We believe that we could not and should not give up on our small towns and expects the rest of our state to prosper and that is what brought me to the high school gym in Lebanon. To announce that we're going to bring over 300 high-tech jobs, jobs that paid twice the county average. One student told a reporter that before this, he always thought he would have to move away to raise his family and find a good job. I just heard from this young man. He is a junior at Virginia tech. His older brother just moved back home to Lebanon. Because there was an information technology job open for him that was too good to pass up. That is a story worth writing all across America. With the right leadership, we can once again achieved the standard of living that is improved and not diminished in each generation. We can once again make America the beacon for science and technology and discovery. Ladies and gentlemen, we know how to do it. The American people ready and Barack Obama and Joe Biden and will get it done. As Governor Of Virginia, it was humbling to occupy a position that was once held by Thomas Jefferson. Almost as daunting is delivering the keynote address four years after Barack Obama. For speaking before Hillary Clinton. -- or speaking before Hillary Clinton. Thomas Jefferson wrote one of his frequent letters to his old rival, John Adams. He complained about the aches of getting old. What was on his mind was what would life be like for the next generation of Americans. As Jefferson was ready to go to sleep, he closed his letter by writing, "I like the dreams of the future better than the history of the past. " Jefferson got it right at the dawn of the century and it is our job to get right at the dawn of the 21st. This race is all about the future and that is why we must Elect Barack Obama as our next president. Because the race for the future will be won when old partisanship gives way to new ideas. When we put solutions over stalemate. And when hope replaces fear. Tonight, looking out and all of you and with a deep faith in the character and resolve of the American people, I'm more confident than ever that we will win that race and make that future ours. Thank you and God bless you and God bless The United States of America.

speaker: Mike Honda
text: My name is Mike Honda. I am a proud sun state Democrat and a silicon valley congressman, but above all, I am a teacher. One of my proudest moments was when I received my first keys to my very first classroom. It was high quality public education that allowed this son of strawberry chair croppers -- strawberry sharecroppers, raised in internment camps of world war II to grow up to be a peace corps volunteer a vice chair of the d.n.c. And chair of the Asian-pacific American caucus. Education is the gateway to the American dream. Barack knows firsthand that immigrant families, Asian, Hispanic, pacific islander, African, and others can attain the American dream through a quality education and hard work. Barack's story of promise and opportunity proves that education can be the great equalizer. Sadly, over the past eight year the misguided Bush-McCain priorities have let our school resources dry, shutting down that gateway to success and hijacking our children's capacity to achieve the American dream. We are now at a crossroads. We can either continue on a path of failed policies with John McCain, or we can change -- seek the change we need when we select Barack Obama as president. America is in dire need of leadership. Barack knows that education leads to innovation, a critical engine of our economy. His ability, his vision will help ensure that everyone has the opportunity to obtain a quality education. Barack will fight for our children's future, rather than burning thru billions of dollars on failed foreign policies and an open-ended war. Barack knows that the real war to be fought is for the education of our children, the future of America's economic health. He is committed to developing the teachers of tomorrow from among the brightest in our classrooms today and preparing, retaining, and rewarding every teacher in America for their service. In 1965, President Kennedy's call to service led me to build schools in El Salvador. Now, though my hair is gray, I am inspired again. With Barack Obama and Joe Biden as our ticket, yes, yes there is hope for the change we need. Thank you, Senator Obama for unlocking the imagination and idealism of all generations of Americans. Including our Asian-pacific islander communities. Thank you for inspiring all of us to be a critical part of your journey to victory this November. And thank you for your commitment to education, for knowing the strength of America's tomorrow lies in the classrooms of today.

speaker: Nancy Floyd
text: Good evening. Every since I graduated college, I have worked in one way or another on renewable energy, trying to find cleaner and cheaper ways for power from everything to our cars into our homes and to our entire economy. I have won a tool belt and I can't put on rattlesnake guards and I have got to my hand 30 and I helped build some of the very first farms and the country and now my partners and I are working work with energy on to brewers and helping to do this. When I graduated college, the entire nation was focused on the energy crisis. Three decades have passed and where are we now? The cost of filling up the gas tanks or cooling our homes are going through the roof. The climate is warming and are reliance on foreign oil has never been greater. Here we are again. We are at another energy crisis. There are thousands of people from my home state of Oregon to my college town in Pennsylvania and they have new solutions and investors are lining up to back them. What is missing is the leadership from Washington. We simply cannot afford another 30 years of partisanship and inaction. We need new thinking and new leadership. We need Barack Obama as the President of the United States. What is at stake is just not the national security and not just the future of our planet, it is whether or not The United States will leave the creation of historic proportions and create millions of high-paying jobs. Let me put it this way. Reed technology is where the computer industry was at in 1984. Think about how far we have come. That is how far reaching and how transformational green technology will be. That's lost jobs here in home. And the loss of one of the biggest opportunities to come away. It is time for change. Barack Obama has a comprehensive energy plan that will get us off foreign oil and will stop global warming and will create millions of jobs in the U.S... I have read it. And you should read it to. At barackobama.com. He is not proposing the same old tired answers. He is offering the change we so urgently need. He will cut taxes for families to buy fuel efficient cars and trucks and by the way make sure that those cars are being built by American auto workers. He will make unprecedented investment in green technologies and that will create 5 million jobs over the next decade. I know to some this may sound high in the sky. I ain't sure a lot of people said that about the home computer and the internet. The truth is America can do it. With the right leadership, we can seize this moment and from Barack Obama as president, we can set our economy on a course for growth and widespread prosperity. Thank you and go Barack Obama.

speaker: Nydia M. Velazquez
text: Good evening my fellow new yorkers. In 2008, the face of our nation's leadership is changing. In every corner of American life. The business world is no longer dominated by country club economics. It is powered by the creative thinking of our interpreters. Nowhere is this more true than when our -- our business owners who own more than half of women business owners and all Americans desperately need change. The Bush administration still refuses to implement laws on the box that give women access to the federal marketplace. Costing them millions in lost opportunities. We cannot afford more of the same. John McCain has already proven to be more of the same. He has consistently opposed opportunities for women in the workforce. Saying they just need training and education. Senator McCain should know that women already earned more advanced degrees than men. Senator McCain should now that we deserve and we will demand a level playing field. John McCain will not just hold that female entrepreneurs. He will hurt all small businesses. He has pledged to continue the Bush tax cuts that favre fortune 500 companies over entrepreneurs. In fact, only 4% of small saw a penny more. Is this practice of neglecting small businesses that has resulted in unemployment rates skyrocketing to a four year high. We cannot afford more of the same. Leave it to the Republicans to serve a big business agenda. They may not know the difference between small -- Barack Obama knows small business is big business in America. Barack Obama will provide small- business tax incentives to encourage investment and spur innovation. He will introduce a health care plan that addresses the unique challenges to small firms. He will give entrepreneurs the tools they need to succeed through programs in the small business administration. Most importantly, he will break the cycle of Republican cronyism that has American business in a chuckle. And it renewed the Contras -- the country's promise so that every American can live the beauty of their dreams. From the single mom from loop -- world appalachia who wants to move from welfare to business ownership, to the Latino in the barrio who is making minimum- wage who wants to be an entrepreneur. Once in a lifetime, the poet said. Once in a lifetime hope and history meet in one extraordinary man and movement. I thank the good lord that I have lived long enough to meet and help such a man twice in my lifetime, John Kennedy and Barack Obama. Kennedy, at 43, proved that age makes a difference in the White House. His energy, his appeal to other young world leaders, his calm under pressure, his openness to new thinking served our nation well. He was denounced when announced as a candidate for lacking experience, he displayed sound judgment in leading a successful nationwide campaign, choosing a top-notch team, negotiating with difficult leaders and out-organizing and out-thinking his adversaries just as he would as president, particularly when, with prunes and courage, he induce -- with prudence and courage, he induced the Soviets to withdraw their missiles from Cuba without The United States firing a shot. And the whole world -- the whole world -- said thanks, that the more experienced Richard Nixon had not been elected in that close election. In 1960, Kennedy, like Obama today, facing a Republican tide, looked to the future of new ideas and opportunities. He did not send the marine corps to liberate American oil supplies, he sent the peace corps to preserve America's standing abroad. And when the Soviets had a military advantage through their lead in space, he made every American proud by literally reaching to the moon. Today, we need new leadership. We've lost our way. We have lost the respect of our allies. We have lost the confidence of our consumers and investors. Are we going to be the first generation to turn our country over to our children in worse shape than we received it in? In short, this year, my friends, the fates will try us, erase all trace of fear and bias. We have the man we need at last to embrace the future, not the past, and dispel eight years of pain and shame, Barack Obama is his name. Call the roll.

speaker: Arthur Davis
text: Thank you, ladies and gentlemen. Thank you, ladies and gentlemen. On behalf of the state of Alabama, where Martin Luther King first took to a pulpit, on behalf of the state of Alabama, where Rosa parks stood up by sitting down, I greet you today . I am honored to second the nomination of the man whose victory tonight takes us closer to becoming what we know America can be, ladies and gentlemen -- a place where who you are now, a place where where you come from, a place where all of the things that may hold you back, pose no permanent barriers, where our destiny is what our God and our dreams determine it to be. Now, ladies and gentlemen, I come from the south, so we like to testify. So I want to tell you, as I second the nomination of Barack Obama, what I have seen with my own eyes. In a cavernous auditorium in Birmingham, Alabama, this past January, I heard Barack Obama tell my state that our generation can find its way again. I heard him tell my state on a sunday afternoon that, if you really want to see a politics of faith and morality, you'll find it in the faith of the least of these and the long among us. I heard him remind my red state that there is no such thing as a Democratic way or a Republican way to build strong families, that work and personal responsibility are Democratic standards, too. And this above all, ladies and gentlemen -- I heard Barack Obama tell my state that to sustain our veterans and protect our soldiers is neither a liberal value nor a conservative value but an American value. Because the men and women who wear the American uniform and fight under the American flag and whose bodies come home in caskets draped with the American flag belong not just to some of us but to all of us. They belong not just to George Bush and Dick Cheney, who heedlessly led us to war in Iraq, but to Barack Obama and Joe Biden, who will begin to bring our soldiers home. And I saw a band of people, 20,000 strong, who don't live together, don't send their children to school together, who have no history of voting together,-linked in a common cause together, ready to take action together. And, ladies and gentlemen, this is the cause for which we stand -- an American President Named Barack Obama, who will lead and inspire the free world, an American President Named Barack Obama, who will stand for the rule of law, who will remember that torture is the way of the people who hate us and not our way, an American President Named Barack Obama, who will affirm that terrorism can never win unless it warps us and makes us forget who we are and what we are. Ladies and gentlemen, 20 years ago, I watched the Democratic convention on a little TV in a tiny motel room in montgomery, Alabama. My mother and grandmother and I were forced to live in that room for three weeks because our home had been fore closed. The fact that I could go from a foreclosure and watching this convention on a tiny hotel room to standing before you in the nation 20 years later, nominating the next President of the United States, tells you very little about me but tells us everything that is right about my country. My fellow Democrats, my fellow Americans, I have never seen a moment like this. I have never seen a sense of urgency like this. I'm 40 years old and I've never seen my country as energized as this. As our next President Has said, from the places in America where people hurt to the places where people dream, our time is now. Our time is now! May God bless you, ladies and gentlemen.

speaker: Beau Biden
text: Good evening. I'm Beau Biden and Joe Biden is my dad. Many of you know him as a distinguished and accomplish center. I know him as an incredible father and loving grandfather. A man who hustled come to Delaware after the last vote so he would not miss me and my brother's games. After returning from work -- some wartorn region of the world he would tiptoe into our room and kiss us good night. He did not want to miss my daughter's birthday. The truth is that he almost was not a Senator At all. In 1972, shortly after his improbable victory, my father went to Washington to look at his new office space. My mom took us to go buy a christmas tree. On the way home, we work in an automobile accident. My mom and my sister were killed. My brother Hunter and I were seriously injured and hospitalized four weeks. I was just short of four years old. One of my first memories was being at the hospital with my dad at our side. We, my brother and I, were all that he cared about. He decided not to take the oath of office. He said that Delaware can get another senator, but my boys can not get another father. However, and great men like ted Kennedy, like mansfield, Hubert Humphrey, men who had been tested in their own right convinced him to serve. He was sworn in in the hospital at my bedside. As a single parent, and he decided to be there to put us to bed. And to be there when we woke up from a bad dream. To make us breakfast. He traveled to and from Washington four hours a day. Five years later, we come up my brother, at that, and I married my mother, Jill. They, together, rebuild our family. 36 years later, he still makes that trip. Even though dad worked in Washington, he has never been part of Washington. He always sounded like the kid from Scranton, pa. That he is. Even that is a story of overcoming. Some people poke fun at my dad talking too much. When he was a young boy, and he had a severe stutter. The kids called him dash. Not because he was fast on the football field, but like a dash at the end of a sentence that you cannot finish. Now he speaks with a clear, strong voice. He says what needs to be said and he does what needs to be done. When domestic violence was often a dark secret, that wrote the violence against women act which gave countless women the protection and support and protection and a new chance at life. When crime was biking in our communities, and dad wrote the crime bill -- spiking in our communities, and dad wrote the crime bill. When Serbian thugs were committing genocide in the balkans, and dad did not hesitate to call Slobodan Milosevic a war criminal to his face. And to convince Congress and our allies to act. He is willing to speak truth to power, to The White House, and to world leaders. I know my father would be a great Vice President. As I mentioned, my dad has always been there for me. My brother and my sister every day. Because of other duties, it would not be possible for me to be here this fall for me to use it -- stand by him the way he stood by me. Be there for my dad like he was for me. Be there for Barack Obama, because our country needs him. Be there for both of them, because millions of families need to know that their best days are not behind them, but ahead of them. Be there for both of them. Millions of people are trying to overcome, just like my dad overcame. Be there because Barack Obama and Joe Biden will deliver America at the change we so desperately need. Please join me in welcoming my friend, my father, my hero, the next Vice President of the United States, and Joe Biden.

speaker: Beth Robinson
text: Good evening. I speak tonight of the wife of the United States marine. Hoo-rah. I am an advocate for military families, a Colorado graduate, and a disabled person living with multiple sclerosis. Most of all, I may proud American. And I see in Senator Obama the leadership, judgment, and acknowledge that will bring these changes that we desperately need to The United States. Senator Obama asked Americans to answer his call to service and I answered. Here I am. As Michele Obama said, military families know the pain of an empty seat at the table. We in your the uncertainty that comes from deployment and war, but we do not complain. We serve on questioning, with devotion, to our proud nation. Military families are grateful for our medical benefits, but I am appalled by the state of our health-care system in our country and frustrated by the limitations of military health care. It is inadequate and underfunded. Barack Obama extended health care benefits for all military spouses in the Senate. He will honor our nation's commitment to military families as president. And I know that President Obama will make health care available and accessible to all Americans. Military families frequently receive orders to move and do so without hesitation. Yet the lack of quality base housing has caused distress and financial burden. The state of our economy has left many service members facing foreclosure or financial ruin. The respect of America for our service members must be reflected in how our government cares for them. Equitable pay and retirement benefits, access to quality housing, health care, and counseling, availability of on- based child care and schools, referral and support services for active duty, reserves, veterans, and their families, are not negotiable. These will be the priorities for Barack Obama as president. Senator Obama recognizes the importance of military families and has asked for our input. Look what they have done to our country. Look what they have done to our planet. It is time to bring our nation back to reality. It is time for an energy policy that recognizes national security needs and dependence on oil and the future is about new ideas and change for the better, not snake oil and quackery. It is time for recognition that threats to our planet are threats to our great country. It is time to understand that in the long run, yes indeed in the short run, we must wean ourselves of addiction to oil. You know, it is time to Elect Barack Obama President of the United States.
